Cross-reference AWS CloudWatch: query VolumeQueueLength and VolumeReadWriteLatency for vol-0a8f3d21c4b9e7f12 — a 4-minute spike in queue depth confirms EBS impairment, not an etcd software bug.
2. Stabilize the cluster: Verify etcd is fully recovered post-impairment: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint status --write-out=table — all 3 members should show the same Raft term and close DB sizes. Confirm API server health: kubectl get --raw /healthz. Let kubelet auto-reconnect — GPU nodes should return Ready within 2–3 minutes of etcd stabilization. Monitor: watch -n5 'kubectl get nodes | grep -c NotReady'. If any node stays NotReady after 8 minutes, SSH in and check: journalctl -u kubelet --since '07:09' | grep -E 'error|timeout|connection refused'. Restart kubelet if it is stuck: systemctl restart kubelet.
3. Recover training jobs: List failed pods: kubectl get pods -n training --field-selector=status.phase=Failed -o wide. Identify checkpoint coverage: for each job, run aws s3 ls s3://ml-training-prod/checkpoints/<job-name>/ --recursive | sort | tail -3. If last checkpoint is older than the job start time, no usable checkpoint exists. Restart each job: kubectl delete job <job-name> -n training && kubectl apply -f jobs/<job-name>.yaml with RESUME_FROM_CHECKPOINT env var set (or unset for epoch-0 restarts). Verify NCCL rendezvous completes: kubectl logs -n training <job-name>-worker-0 | grep -E 'NCCL INFO Bootstrap|Resuming from checkpoint step'.
4. Root cause confirmation — gp2 vs io1: aws ec2 describe-volumes --volume-ids vol-0a8f3d21c4b9e7f12 --query 'Volumes[0].VolumeType' → 'gp2'. Pull Terraform git history: git log --all --oneline -- terraform/modules/controlplane/main.tf | head -5 — reveals February commit changing volume_type from 'io1' to 'gp2' and removing the iops field. The etcd documentation (etcd.io/docs/v3.5/op-guide/hardware) explicitly states: 'etcd requires a low latency disk. WAL fsyncs block the Raft heartbeat. SSD is strongly recommended.' gp2 volumes are subject to burst-bucket depletion and AWS-side noisy-neighbor I/O impairments — neither affects dedicated io1/io2 provisioned IOPS.
5. Durable fix — restore dedicated IOPS, raise election tolerance: Option A (live migration, no restart): Modify the EBS volume in-place — aws ec2 modify-volume --volume-id vol-0a8f3d21c4b9e7f12 --volume-type io2 --iops 4000. Change is hot and takes effect within minutes. Then reconcile Terraform state: terraform import aws_ebs_volume.cp2_etcd vol-0a8f3d21c4b9e7f12. Repeat for cp-1 and cp-3 volumes. Option B (lower fsync latency): Replace control plane EC2 instances with c5d.2xlarge (NVMe instance store, ~0.1ms fsync, immune to EBS events). Rolling replace: drain → etcd member remove → terminate → new node → etcd member add → verify quorum. Option C (defense-in-depth — increase etcd election tolerance): Add --heartbeat-interval=500 --election-timeout=5000 to the etcd systemd unit flags. This tolerates single-digit-second I/O hiccups without triggering elections while still detecting genuine failure in <5s.
6. Prevent: (a) Prometheus alert on WAL fsync latency: alert when etcd_disk_wal_fsync_duration_seconds{quantile='0.99'} > 0.05 for 60s → PagerDuty P2 'etcd disk pressure' — fires ~8 minutes before election timeouts at this threshold. (b) Alert on leadership instability: alert when increase(etcd_server_leader_changes_seen_total[1h]) > 1 → P1 'etcd leader election detected' — any election in a week is anomalous. (c) Terraform Sentinel policy: aws_ebs_volume resources in the control-plane module must have volume_type in ['io2', 'io1'] — enforced in Atlantis PR plan checks, fails CI on violation. (d) AWS CloudWatch alarm: VolumeQueueLength > 0.5 for any control plane EBS volume for >60s → PagerDuty P2 'Control plane EBS pressure'. (e) Add to new-cluster runbook and SRE onboarding: 'etcd requires dedicated low-latency storage. gp2 and st1 are explicitly unsupported. Validate with: fio --name=wal --rw=write --bs=4k --ioengine=sync --fsync=1 --runtime=60s -- expect <10ms p99.'

items.drill: cert-manager CertificateRequest Backlog — 1-Minute MLflow Cert TTL Saturates Vault PKI, Istiod Webhook TLS Expires, Blocks All GKE Pod Scheduling Cluster-Wide for 23 Minutes
items.layer: Infrastructure (Kubernetes 1.30 / GKE Standard / Istio 1.22.1 / cert-manager 1.14.7 / HashiCorp Vault 1.17.0 / Go / Python)
items.scenario:
At 11:23:14 UTC, PagerDuty fires seven simultaneous P1 alerts within 4 seconds: 'llm-gateway-prod pod-availability 0/8 (threshold: 6)', 'recommendation-api pod-availability 2/6', 'payment-svc-prod pod-availability 3/12', 'monitoring-prod grafana pod 0/1', plus three additional service degradation alerts. Multiple unrelated namespaces failing at the same second is the first diagnostic signal.

Cluster: prod-ml-gke-us-central1 (GKE Standard 1.30.3-gke.1225001, 12 node pools, 156 nodes n2-standard-16, Google Cloud us-central1). Istio 1.22.1, cert-manager 1.14.7, HashiCorp Vault 1.17.0 running on 3 dedicated n2-highcpu-16 nodes.

kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -30 (11:23:17 UTC):

  NAMESPACE             REASON         MESSAGE
  llm-gateway-prod      FailedCreate   error creating: Internal error occurred: failed calling webhook
                                       "namespace.sidecar-injector.istio.io":
                                       Post "https://istiod.istio-system.svc:443/inject?timeout=10s":
                                       x509: certificate has expired or is not yet valid:
                                       current time 2026-07-01T11:23:14Z is after
                                       2026-07-01T11:21:47Z
  payment-svc-prod      FailedCreate   [identical webhook error - 11:23:15 UTC]
  recommendation-api    FailedCreate   [identical webhook error - 11:23:16 UTC]
  monitoring-prod       FailedCreate   [identical webhook error - 11:23:17 UTC]

All pod scheduling failures share the same error: expired TLS certificate on the Istio sidecar injector webhook (istiod.istio-system.svc:443). The MutatingWebhookConfiguration 'istio-sidecar-injector' has failurePolicy: Fail — any webhook TLS failure returns HTTP 500 from the API server, blocking pod creation cluster-wide.

kubectl get pods -n istio-system (11:24 UTC):
  istiod-6f9b4c8d7-x4k2p   1/1   Running   0   14d
  istiod-6f9b4c8d7-m7n8q   1/1   Running   0   14d

Istiod pods are healthy — the issue is the TLS cert istiod uses to serve the webhook endpoint, managed by cert-manager.

kubectl get certificaterequest -n istio-system (11:24 UTC):
  NAME                        APPROVED   DENIED   READY   ISSUER       AGE
  istiod-cert-r97bx           True       False    False   vault-pki    39m   ← stuck
  istiod-cert-r94mc           True       False    False   vault-pki    40m   ← also stuck

kubectl describe certificaterequest istiod-cert-r97bx -n istio-system:
  Status:
    Conditions:
      Type: Ready, Status: False, Reason: Pending
      Message: Waiting for approval from Vault PKI: POST
               https://vault.vault-system.svc:8200/v1/pki_int/sign/istio-service
               returned: 503 Service Unavailable (rate limited: 10014 active
               requests exceed max_request_duration 2s, throttle_queue_length=4127)

HashiCorp Vault PKI metrics (vault-system namespace, 11:24 UTC):
  vault_core_request_duration_p99: 8,412ms (normal: 12ms)
  vault_core_active_requests: 14,204 (max_request_duration triggers at 10,000)
  vault_pki_sign_requests_total rate: 520/s (baseline: 3.2/s)

Root cause: At 10:44:18 UTC, an ML platform team member created a cert-manager Issuer for MLflow experiment tracking pods (issuer.spec.vault.path: pki_int/sign/mlflow-ephemeral) with duration: 1m (1-minute TTL) to comply with a draft security policy for 'ephemeral workloads'. A Helm chart deployed 1,847 MLflow experiment pods across the cluster starting at 10:44 UTC. Each pod requests a new TLS cert every 57 seconds (cert-manager renews at 95% of TTL). Steady-state PKI load: 1,847 × (60/57) = 1,944 sign requests/minute = 32.4 sign requests/second. Over 39 minutes this consumed 75,882 PKI operations, saturating Vault's request queue (configured for low-volume internal PKI use at 10,000 concurrent requests with 2-second timeout). cert-manager's CertificateRequest objects for ALL issuers share the same Vault connection pool — MLflow cert saturation starved the istiod-cert renewal of Vault API capacity. istiod-cert (normal 1-hour TTL, renewal starts at T-30m = 10:51 UTC) submitted 39 renewal attempts against a saturated Vault, all timing out, until the cert expired at 11:21:47 UTC. Kubernetes MutatingWebhook with failurePolicy: Fail then blocked every new pod across all namespaces.

Impact timeline:
  10:44:18 UTC — MLflow 1-minute TTL cert Helm chart deployed to prod
  10:51:00 UTC — cert-manager begins istiod-cert renewal (T-30m, normal)
  10:51:03 UTC — first Vault timeout on istiod-cert renewal (503, queue backlog)
  11:21:47 UTC — istiod-cert TLS certificate hard-expires
  11:21:47 UTC — all new pod scheduling begins failing (failurePolicy: Fail)
  11:23:14 UTC — PagerDuty fires (on existing pod-availability monitors)
  11:23:17 UTC — on-call identifies webhook error in kubectl get events
  11:46:32 UTC — full resolution

Business impact: 23-minute cluster-wide pod scheduling freeze. 0 new pods could be created or replaced in any namespace. 6 in-progress deployment rollouts stalled (including a payment service security patch). 38 ML training jobs could not scale worker pods. HPA could not scale any deployment up during a traffic spike. Revenue impact: $190K from payment service scaling failure during 23-minute period.
items.exercise:
1. Triage — distinguish cluster-wide scheduling failure from node failure or API server outage: kubectl cluster-info — API server healthy. kubectl get nodes | grep -c NotReady — 0 (all nodes Ready). kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase=Pending | wc -l — expect to show hundreds of Pending pods across all namespaces (cluster-wide, not isolated to one namespace). This rules out a node or namespace issue. Run: kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | grep FailedCreate | head -5 — all events show the same webhook error 'x509: certificate has expired'. Webhook name 'namespace.sidecar-injector.istio.io' identifies istiod as the failing component. Verify istiod is alive (kubectl get pods -n istio-system) — istiod pods Running. Root cause is the webhook TLS cert, not the istiod process itself.
2. Immediate mitigation (30 seconds) — set failurePolicy: Ignore temporarily: kubectl patch mutatingwebhookconfiguration istio-sidecar-injector --type=json -p '[{"op":"replace","path":"/webhooks/0/failurePolicy","value":"Ignore"}]'. This immediately allows all pod scheduling to resume without sidecar injection (pods will not receive the Envoy sidecar). Verify: kubectl apply -f test-pod.yaml succeeds within 5 seconds. WARNING: pods created during this window will NOT have the Istio sidecar — they will bypass mTLS policy and cannot participate in the service mesh. Communicate immediately to the on-call team that mTLS is suspended for new pods until the cert is restored.
3. Kill the cert storm — scale MLflow experiment pods to zero: kubectl scale deployment -l app=mlflow-experiment -A --replicas=0. This immediately drops Vault PKI load from 32.4 sign requests/second back to 3.2/second baseline. Verify: vault_core_active_requests drops from 14,204 → <200 within 90 seconds (check via kubectl exec -n vault-system vault-0 -- vault status). Once Vault request queue clears, cert-manager will be able to complete the pending istiod-cert CertificateRequest.
4. Restore istiod webhook TLS cert: cert-manager will automatically retry the istiod-cert CertificateRequest once Vault is unblocked. Monitor: kubectl get certificaterequest -n istio-system -w — expect istiod-cert-r97bx to show Ready=True within 60-90 seconds of Vault recovering. Once cert is issued: kubectl rollout restart deployment/istiod -n istio-system — istiod restarts with the new cert and begins serving the webhook with valid TLS. Restore failurePolicy: Fail: kubectl patch mutatingwebhookconfiguration istio-sidecar-injector --type=json -p '[{"op":"replace","path":"/webhooks/0/failurePolicy","value":"Fail"}]'. Rollout restart any deployments that were created without sidecars during the Ignore window (they need re-injection): kubectl rollout restart deployment -A -l app!=mlflow-experiment.
5. Root fix — prohibit sub-5-minute cert TTLs in cert-manager: Add a ValidatingWebhookConfiguration or OPA/Gatekeeper policy that rejects Certificate resources with spec.duration < 5m: rego rule `deny[msg] { input.kind == "Certificate"; duration_seconds := time.parse_duration_ns(input.spec.duration) / 1e9; duration_seconds < 300; msg := sprintf("Certificate TTL %vs is below minimum 300s (5m). Short-lived certs saturate PKI issuers.", [duration_seconds])}`. This would have blocked the MLflow cert issuer at creation time. Additionally, add cert-manager resource isolation: configure separate VaultConnection resources for infrastructure certs (istiod, ingress-nginx, monitoring) vs workload certs (MLflow, experiment pods) using dedicated Vault roles with separate connection pools and request quotas.
6. Validate: Deploy the OPA policy, then attempt: kubectl apply -f mlflow-cert-1m.yaml — should return 'Error: certificate TTL 1m violates minimum policy (300s)'. Verify istiod webhook health: kubectl exec -n istio-system istiod-<pod> -- openssl s_client -connect localhost:443 -showcerts 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -enddate — confirm certificate valid for 1h. Run 50 pod scheduling operations concurrently: kubectl create job cert-test --image=alpine -- sleep 5 (repeated) — all should complete in <3s with sidecars injected. Confirm PKI baseline: vault_core_request_duration_p99 returns to <15ms.
7. Prevent: (a) Add cert-manager CertificateRequest age alert: Prometheus alert when max(certmanager_certificaterequest_ready_transition_seconds{reason!='Issued'}) > 300 (5 minutes pending) for any CertificateRequest → PagerDuty P2 'cert-manager CertificateRequest stuck'. Would have fired at 10:56 UTC (5 minutes into istiod-cert starvation), 25 minutes before TLS expiry. (b) Add istiod webhook cert expiry alert: alert when (certmanager_certificate_expiration_timestamp_seconds{name='istiod-cert',namespace='istio-system'} - time()) < 1800 (30 minutes) → PagerDuty P1. A 30-minute window provides time to diagnose and recover before expiry. (c) Use failurePolicy: Ignore with a network policy compensating control: switch istiod MutatingWebhookConfiguration to failurePolicy: Ignore and enforce Istio mTLS via AuthorizationPolicy (require ServiceAccount identity, not sidecar presence). This eliminates the cluster-wide scheduling bomb while maintaining security posture. (d) Add a pre-upgrade validation for any cert-manager Issuer changes: CI step validates `min(certificate.spec.duration) >= '5m'` across all Chart.yaml manifests before deploying to production. (e) Separate Vault connection pools per cert tier: dedicated Vault AppRole for infra-tier certs (istiod, ingress, monitoring) with unlimited request_duration; workload-tier certs use a separate AppRole with rate_limit_window='1m' and max_requests=1000. A saturated workload pool cannot starve the infra pool.

items.drill: Triton Inference Server Silent Fallback — Model Config/Weight Shape Mismatch
items.layer: ML Serving (Triton / AWS EKS / SageMaker Feature Store)
items.scenario:
At 09:47 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'fraud-model-triton error_rate 12.3% (threshold: 2%)'. Your Triton Inference Server cluster (6× NVIDIA T4-16GB on AWS EKS, us-east-1) serves a fraud detection ONNX model at /v2/models/fraud_detector/infer. The model was automatically retrained overnight (weekly cycle) and new weights uploaded to s3://ml-models-prod/fraud_detector/v2.3.1/. Triton polls S3 every 30 min for hot-reloads. Triton logs flood with:

  E0409 09:03:14.221847 1 model_repository_manager.cc:1741] failed to load 'fraud_detector' version 3: [StatusCode::INVALID_ARGUMENT] model config input 'input__0': the model expect tensor shape [1,512] but the configuration specified shape [1,256]
  E0409 09:03:14.221859 1 model_repository_manager.cc:1744] ModelRepositoryManager::LoadModels() failed for model 'fraud_detector'

Triton silently fell back to v2.3.0 (old weights, 256-dim input), but the upstream SageMaker Feature Store pipeline was already migrated to produce 512-dim feature vectors for v2.3.1. 12% of requests hit a code path that passes new vectors to the old model, causing shape mismatch errors. The remaining 88% still hit the legacy feature path and serve stale predictions from a 6-day-old model. Business alert: chargeback rate jumped from 0.8% → 1.2% ($280K daily revenue impact). The CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions) shows the model export job succeeded but config.pbtxt was never updated — a shape detection bug in the config generation script failed silently on dynamic ONNX axes.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Check nv_inference_request_failure and nv_model_load_duration_us in Prometheus. GET /v2/models/fraud_detector to confirm which version is READY (expect v2 = READY, v3 = UNAVAILABLE). Check CloudWatch Logs for S3 sync events — confirm weights arrived but config update failed.
2. Immediate mitigation: Roll back the SageMaker Feature Group to produce 256-dim vectors (flip feature pipeline version env var, redeploy SageMaker Processing Job). This restores consistency with the old weights. Error rate should drop to ~0% within 2 min as new feature batches flow through.
3. Root cause: Inspect config.pbtxt in s3://ml-models-prod/fraud_detector/v2.3.1/ — input shape still shows [1,256]. Review generate_config.py in CI: the dynamic axis detection calls onnx.checker.check_model() but reads input shape from the first symbolic dim, which is 'batch_size', not the feature dim. Bug introduced in commit a3f8d2c.
4. Fix: Correct generate_config.py to read input shape from dim[1] instead of dim[0]. Regenerate config.pbtxt with correct shape [1,512], upload both config and weights to S3. Trigger manual reload: POST /v2/repository/models/fraud_detector/load (body: {"parameters": {"config": ""}}). Wait for model version 3 status = READY.
5. Validate: GET /v2/models/fraud_detector/versions/3 → READY. Re-enable 512-dim feature pipeline. Confirm nv_inference_request_failure drops to 0. Monitor chargeback rate returns to 0.8% baseline within 2–4 hours.
6. Prevent: Add model config validation step in GitHub Actions before S3 upload — compare all config.pbtxt input/output shapes against actual ONNX graph shapes using onnx.load().graph.input[i].type.tensor_type.shape. Alert on any Triton model load failure event (not just on inference errors), and add a liveness check that validates the expected model version is READY after every retraining pipeline run.

items.drill: vLLM KV-Cache Exhaustion Under Traffic Burst
items.layer: ML Serving (vLLM / NVIDIA A100)
items.scenario: At 14:23 PST, PagerDuty fires: 'llm-serving-prod error rate 28.4% (threshold: 5%)'. Your vLLM cluster (4× A100-80GB, GKE) serves Llama-3-70B via /v1/completions. Grafana shows `vllm:gpu_kv_cache_usage_perc` at 99.8% across all nodes, `vllm:num_queued_requests` spiking to 340. Logs flood with: `torch.cuda.OutOfMemoryError: CUDA out of memory. Tried to allocate 2.34 GiB (GPU 0; 79.20 GiB total capacity; 71.42 GiB already allocated; 2.01 GiB free; 72.18 GiB reserved in total by PyTorch)`. Concurrency jumped from 18→52 req/s after a marketing email blast at 14:15 PST. P99 latency: 1.2s → 47s timeout. Model weights (35 GB) are fine — KV cache for in-flight sequences consumed the remaining VRAM.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Query Prometheus for vllm:num_running_requests, vllm:num_queued_requests, vllm:gpu_kv_cache_usage_perc — confirm KV cache is the bottleneck, not model weights or CUDA fragmentation
2. Hotfix: Patch vLLM deployment ConfigMap — reduce max_num_seqs from 256→48 and max_num_batched_tokens from 8192→4096. Rolling-restart pods one at a time (kubectl rollout restart, 1 unavailable) to preserve partial capacity
3. Load shedding: Apply nginx rate limit (limit_req_zone, 40 r/s burst=20) at the Istio ingress gateway to prevent re-saturation during restarts
4. Validate: KV cache utilization stabilizes at ~65%, P99 returns to 1.6s, error rate < 0.8% within 8 minutes of first pod restart completing
5. Root fix: Calculate correct max_num_seqs = floor((total_gpu_mem - model_weights_mem - overhead) / (max_model_len × num_layers × head_dim × 2 × dtype_bytes)). Add HPA rule: scale out when vllm:num_queued_requests > 15 for 60s. Add pre-scaling hook tied to marketing campaign scheduler.

items.drill: Ray Serve Head Node Preemption — Stale GCS Actor Handles Zombie All GPU Workers
items.layer: ML Serving (Ray Serve / Azure AKS / Python)
items.scenario:
At 03:18 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'mistral-inference-prod error_rate 100% for 6 minutes (threshold: 5%)'. Your Ray 2.10.0 cluster (1 head node + 8 worker nodes on Azure AKS, Standard_NC24ads_A100_v4 spot instances, eastus2) serves a fine-tuned Mistral-7B-Instruct model via Ray Serve at /v1/chat/completions. At 03:12:07 UTC, Azure preempted the head node spot VM (30-second IMDS eviction notice received at 03:11:37 UTC, missed by on-call). AKS restarted the head pod in 72 seconds. Serve controller log:

  2026-04-21T03:12:07Z WARN  RayletClient - Lost connection to GCS at ray-head-svc.ray-system.svc.cluster.local:6379
  2026-04-21T03:13:19Z INFO  GcsClient - Reconnected to GCS at ray-head-svc.ray-system.svc.cluster.local:6379 (new epoch: 15)
  2026-04-21T03:13:22Z ERROR ServeController - Failed to recover deployment mistral_serve: ActorID b7f2a3c18f4e1d20... is unreachable
  2026-04-21T03:14:05Z ERROR ServeController - Replica mistral_serve#replica_0: actor handle stale (registered GCS epoch 14, current epoch 15)
  2026-04-21T03:14:05Z ERROR ServeController - Replica mistral_serve#replica_1: actor handle stale (registered GCS epoch 14, current epoch 15)
  [6 more identical lines for replicas 2–7]

All 8 workers still hold PyTorch CUDA contexts from GCS epoch 14. nvidia-smi on every worker shows zombie python processes: GPU-Mem=36400MiB/40960MiB, GPU-Util=0%, State=C. New actor scheduling fails: ray.exceptions.GetTimeoutError: Failed to submit task to actor b7f2a3c1... after 30.0s: No healthy replicas. Ray Dashboard: mistral_serve status=UNHEALTHY, running_replicas=0, pending_replicas=8 (stuck STARTING for 4+ min). 14,200 active WebSocket sessions receive 503 ServiceUnavailable. Business impact: $22,800 revenue loss on a $0.08/request pay-per-use API; SLA breach for 3 enterprise customers.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: kubectl get pods -n ray-system — head pod shows RestartCount=1, Running for 5 min. kubectl exec -it ray-head-0 -n ray-system -- ray status — reports 8 nodes registered, 0 live actors. On each worker: kubectl exec -it ray-worker-0 -n ray-system -- nvidia-smi — confirms zombie python pids with 36.4 GB allocated but 0% GPU compute. This proves GCS epoch mismatch: workers hold epoch-14 actor PIDs that no longer exist in the head's fresh epoch-15 registry.
2. Immediate mitigation: kubectl rollout restart daemonset/ray-worker -n ray-system. Each worker restart kills zombie python processes (releasing 36.4 GB GPU memory per node) and re-registers with GCS epoch 15. Ray Serve controller detects newly healthy workers and schedules 8 fresh replicas. Mistral weights reload from Azure Blob Storage model cache: ~12s if cached on node, ~35s for cold nodes.
3. Validate: Watch replica count recover: watch -n 5 'kubectl exec ray-head-0 -n ray-system -- ray status | grep running_replicas'. Confirm it climbs 0→1→...→8 over 3–4 minutes. Send a test request: curl -s -X POST https://mistral-prod.eastus2.cloudapp.azure.com/v1/chat/completions -d '{"model":"mistral-7b-instruct","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}]}' — expect 200 OK with valid JSON. Error rate drops to 0 within 30s of 8th replica becoming HEALTHY.
4. Root cause: Ray GCS state was stored in-memory only (RAY_gcs_storage=memory, the default). On head pod restart, GCS starts fresh with epoch 15. Workers still running hold Python object references (actor handles) to specific GCS-epoch-14 remote process PIDs — those PIDs are gone. Ray 2.10 does not automatically evict cross-epoch zombie actors from worker CUDA contexts; only a worker-process restart flushes them. The IMDS eviction notice at 03:11:37 went unmonitored, giving 30s that was not used to drain traffic.
5. Long-term fix — Redis GCS persistence: Configure KubeRay RayCluster CR with a Redis sidecar on the head pod and set RAY_gcs_storage=redis, GCS_REDIS_ADDRESS=localhost:6379. With GCS persistence enabled, head pod restart replays GCS state — workers re-register with the same epoch, actors recover in-place in <10s with zero GPU memory teardown.
6. Long-term fix — Evict head from spot pool: Migrate the head node pool to on-demand (az aks nodepool update --name rayhead --priority Regular --resource-group ml-prod-rg --cluster-name aks-ml-east2). Worker pools remain spot to save cost; head node stability eliminates the #1 GCS state-loss vector at ~$180/month extra.
7. Prevent: (a) Add Azure Monitor alert on IMDS scheduledevents API returning EventType=Preempt targeting head node pool — fire PagerDuty P2 with 25s lead time to trigger graceful drain. (b) Add synthetic health check every 60s: curl /v1/models and assert 200 OK + running_replicas=8 — alert on first failure, not user 503s. (c) Add Grafana alert: ray_serve_deployment_replica_running_replicas{deployment='mistral_serve'} < 8 for 60s → PagerDuty P1. (d) Add CI regression test: kubectl delete pod -l ray.io/node-type=head and assert serve recovery within 3 min (Redis GCS) or 5 min (worker rollout fallback).

items.drill: TorchServe Worker Zombie — CUDA Context Poisoned by OOV Embedding Lookup
items.layer: ML Serving (TorchServe / AWS SageMaker)
items.scenario:
At 08:44 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'recommendation-endpoint-prod InvocationModelErrors 100% for 4 minutes (threshold: 2%)'. Your SageMaker real-time endpoint (ml.g4dn.2xlarge, 1× NVIDIA T4-16GB, TorchServe 0.9.0, model: RecoBERT-v2.1 product recommendation) has served cleanly for 4 months. CloudWatch dashboard shows: ModelLatency=0ms, GPUUtilization=0%, GPUMemoryUtilization=92.5% (14.8 GB / 16 GB — still fully allocated), CPUUtilization=0.3%. Container logs flood with:

  2026-04-17T08:43:11Z [ERROR] BaseHandler - CUDA error: device-side assert triggered at model forward pass: index 3902 is out of bounds for dimension 0 with size 3800
  2026-04-17T08:43:11Z [ERROR] WorkerThread - RuntimeError: CUDA error: device-side assert triggered
  2026-04-17T08:43:12Z [INFO] ManagementRequestHandler - Worker recovery: restarting pid=2844
  2026-04-17T08:43:13Z [ERROR] Worker-2 pid=2847 - RuntimeError: Failed to initialize CUDA context: CUDA error 710 (cudaErrorContextIsDestroyed): context is destroyed
  2026-04-17T08:43:15Z [ERROR] Worker-3 pid=2851 - RuntimeError: Failed to initialize CUDA context: CUDA error 710 (cudaErrorContextIsDestroyed): context is destroyed

Worker pid=2844 hit a CUDA device-side assert when a product category embedding lookup used index 3902 — beyond RecoBERT-v2.1's vocabulary size of 3800. The triggering event: an upstream catalog service deployed 103 new product category IDs at 08:43:10 UTC, expanding from category_id 3799 to 3902 without notifying downstream ML consumers. TorchServe's recovery logic spawned 3 new workers, but the CUDA context for cuda:0 entered a fatal error state. New workers cannot create a fresh CUDA context on the same device. Zombie pid=2844 holds 14.8 GB GPU memory but shows no active PID in nvidia-smi (process is Z/defunct). The endpoint serves zero successful requests for 22 minutes. Business impact: 340K recommendation API calls failed during peak morning traffic; A/B test results corrupted for 3 running experiments.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Query CloudWatch Logs Insights: `fields @timestamp, @message | filter @message like /index.*out of bounds/ | sort @timestamp asc | limit 1` — pinpoints first CUDA assert at 08:43:11. Cross-reference with upstream catalog service deployment event at 08:43:10 (check ECS deploy history or Spinnaker pipeline). Confirm zombie memory signature: GPUMemoryUtilization > 80% with GPUUtilization < 5% for > 3 min.
2. Immediate mitigation: SageMaker doesn't support per-container process restart — trigger endpoint update to cycle the fleet: `aws sagemaker update-endpoint --endpoint-name recommendation-endpoint-prod --endpoint-config-name <current-config>`. This destroys the poisoned CUDA context and releases 14.8 GB. Expect ~90s of full downtime while new containers start.
3. Root cause: RecoBERT-v2.1 embedding layer is `nn.Embedding(3800, 256)` in model.py. Any input with category_id > 3799 triggers an unhandled CUDA bounds error. `CUDA error: device-side assert triggered` is non-recoverable — it permanently corrupts the CUDA context on that device, making all subsequent CUDA allocations fail with error 710 (cudaErrorContextIsDestroyed). The upstream catalog service had no ML schema contract defining the valid category_id range.
4. Short-term fix: Add bounds clamping in the TorchServe handler's `preprocess()` method before forward pass: `category_ids = torch.clamp(category_ids, 0, EMBEDDING_VOCAB_SIZE - 1)`. Alternatively, map OOV IDs to a dedicated `<UNK>` token (ID=0). Deploy via `aws sagemaker create-model` + `update-endpoint-weights-and-capacities` for zero-downtime model swap.
5. Prevent: (a) Add a custom TorchServe health check handler that runs a forward pass with synthetic valid inputs every 30s — if CUDA context is poisoned, health check fails and SageMaker auto-replaces the instance before manual intervention is needed. (b) Publish a `MAX_CATEGORY_ID` contract to AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store; add a pre-deploy validation step in the catalog service CI that reads this param and rejects deploys that would exceed it. (c) Add CloudWatch alarm: GPUUtilization < 5% AND GPUMemoryUtilization > 80% for 3 consecutive minutes → PagerDuty P2 'zombie GPU context' alert.

items.drill: vLLM Speculative Decoding Draft Model Vocab Mismatch — Corrupted Responses After Target Model Rollout
items.layer: ML Serving (vLLM 0.4.2 / GKE Autopilot / NVIDIA A100-80GB)
items.scenario:
At 19:23 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'llm-gateway-prod synthetic-quality-monitor p99-response-length 8 tokens (threshold: 80 tokens) for 8 minutes'. Your vLLM 0.4.2 cluster (6× A100-80GB on GKE Autopilot, us-central1-a) serves Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct at https://llm-gateway-prod.internal/v1 at 3,100 RPS. Speculative decoding is enabled: draft model=Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (vocab_size=32,000), num_speculative_tokens=5. An Argo CD sync at 19:15 UTC upgraded the target model to Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct-v2 (expanded multilingual tokenizer: 32,000→36,864 vocabulary). The draft model image was NOT updated — still vocab_size=32,000.

vLLM container logs (kubectl logs llm-serving-6d9f7-0 -n inference --since=15m):

  2026-05-06T19:21:07Z INFO  vllm.engine.llm_engine - Spec decode acceptance: 0/5 (0.0%) seq_id=91847 [draft=llama-3-1-8b-instruct target=llama-3-1-70b-instruct-v2]
  2026-05-06T19:21:08Z WARN  vllm.spec_decode.batch_expansion - SpecDecodeWorker: rejection on position 4 (last proposal token) — output_token_ids buffer ptr advanced without EOS consume [seq_id=91848]
  2026-05-06T19:21:09Z INFO  vllm.engine.llm_engine - Generated 7 tokens for seq_id=91849 (stop_reason=EOS) duration=88ms
  2026-05-06T19:21:09Z INFO  vllm.engine.llm_engine - Generated 9 tokens for seq_id=91850 (stop_reason=EOS) duration=91ms
  [repeating across all 6 pods]

Prometheus at 19:23 UTC:
  vllm:spec_decode_draft_acceptance_rate{model="llama-3-1-70b-instruct-v2"} = 0.00  (baseline: 0.71)
  vllm:generation_tokens_per_second = 290  (baseline: 4,180)
  vllm:num_running_requests = 240
  vllm:request_success_total rate = 3,100/s  (all HTTP 200 OK — no application-layer errors visible)

Sample API response: {"choices":[{"message":{"content":"certainly! photosynthesis occurs when"}}]} — 6 tokens, mid-sentence truncation.

Root cause — two compounding failures: (1) Token IDs 32,000–36,863 exist in the new tokenizer's multilingual expansion. The 8B draft model has an embedding matrix of size 32,000 — any proposal requiring one of these IDs results in 100% speculation rejection. In vLLM 0.4.2, rejection on the last speculative token slot (idx=4 = num_speculative_tokens−1) fails to reset the output_token_ids write pointer after advancing it for the rejected token. The next sequence begins writing at the already-advanced position, reading trailing garbage from the previous sequence's buffer and triggering a spurious EOS after 7–9 tokens. (2) The Argo CD Application manifest defines target and draft model as separate image tags in two different ConfigMap keys (TARGET_MODEL_IMAGE and SPEC_DECODE_MODEL_IMAGE). There is no deployment-time assertion that the two tokenizer vocabularies match.

14,800 API calls returned truncated 6–9 token responses before the synthetic quality monitor fired. Three enterprise customers on B2B SLAs triggered automated incident escalations. Business impact: $96K in SLA penalty credits; ML team's live A/B experiment results invalidated.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Confirm spec-decode as root cause — check vllm:spec_decode_draft_acceptance_rate in Grafana: dropped to 0.00 at 19:15 UTC (correlates with Argo CD sync). Sample 5 live responses: for i in $(seq 5); do curl -s -X POST https://llm-gateway-prod.internal/v1/chat/completions -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"model":"llama-3-1-70b-instruct-v2","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Explain the water cycle in 200 words"}],"max_tokens":300}' | jq '.choices[0].message.content | length'; done — observe values consistently <15. Confirm vocab mismatch: kubectl exec llm-serving-6d9f7-0 -n inference -- python3 -c "from transformers import AutoTokenizer; d=AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('/models/draft'); t=AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('/models/target'); print('draft', d.vocab_size, 'target', t.vocab_size)" — prints draft 32000 target 36864.
2. Immediate mitigation: Disable speculative decoding — kubectl set env deployment/llm-serving -n inference VLLM_SPECULATIVE_MODEL='' VLLM_NUM_SPECULATIVE_TOKENS=0 then kubectl rollout restart deployment/llm-serving -n inference (max-unavailable=1). Each pod takes ~45s to reload weights from GCS. Throughput drops 4,180→1,200 tok/s but responses are correct. Confirm fix: after first restarted pod becomes Ready, re-run the 5-sample curl test — assert response word count >100. Update Argo CD Application to sync-paused to prevent auto-reverting the env override.
3. Root cause — vLLM 0.4.2 engine bug: In spec_decode/batch_expansion.py, the output buffer write pointer is incremented when a draft token is tentatively appended during proposal scoring. On full rejection (all 5 slots), the rollback path correctly resets pointer for positions 0–3 but skips position 4 (off-by-one in the rollback loop bound). This causes the pointer to remain advanced by one token position for the next sequence. The fix is upstream PR #4899 ('Fix off-by-one in spec decode rejection rollback loop') merged in vLLM 0.4.3.
4. Fix: Upgrade vLLM from 0.4.2 to 0.4.3+: update the container image tag in the Argo CD Application spec from vllm/vllm-openai:v0.4.2 to vllm/vllm-openai:v0.4.3. Simultaneously update the draft model to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-v2 (vocab_size=36,864, matching target). Re-enable speculative decoding with the corrected model pair. Validate via vllm-benchmark: python -m vllm.entrypoints.benchmark_throughput --model /models/target --speculative-model /models/draft --num-prompts 200 --output-len 200 — assert median output_len>190 tokens and spec_decode_draft_acceptance_rate>0.60.
5. Validate: After full rollout, confirm vllm:spec_decode_draft_acceptance_rate returns to 0.65–0.75 (slight drop from 0.71 baseline expected as 8B-v2 adapts to expanded vocab distribution). Replay 50 logged failing requests from Cloud Logging (filter: resource.type=k8s_container, jsonPayload.seq_id in [91847..92200]) against the fixed endpoint — assert all responses exceed 100 tokens. Run 24h shadow traffic comparison: A/B 5% canary with fixed build vs production.
6. Prevent: (a) Add Argo CD PreSync hook that asserts vocab parity: annotate the Rollout with argocd.argoproj.io/hook: PreSync — hook script: python3 /scripts/validate_model_pair.py which runs AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained() for both target and draft and asserts vocab_size equality; sync fails on mismatch. (b) Add Grafana alert: vllm:spec_decode_draft_acceptance_rate < 0.20 for 90s → PagerDuty P1 'spec-decode model mismatch'. (c) Add a synthetic quality canary: every 60s POST a deterministic prompt 'Count from 1 to 30:' and assert len(response.split()) > 25 — this fires within 60s vs the 8-minute gap in this incident. (d) Store SPEC_DECODE_MODEL_PAIR as a single Kubernetes annotation on the Deployment (e.g., 'llama-3-1-70b-instruct-v2:llama-3-1-8b-instruct-v2') — ArgoCD diff surfaces mismatches visually on every rollout plan review.

items.drill: NCCL AllReduce Deadlock — Tensor Parallel vLLM Silent Hang After EFA Driver Upgrade
items.layer: ML Serving (vLLM 0.5.1 / AWS p4d.24xlarge / 8× A100-80GB / EFA Networking)
items.scenario:
At 22:47 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'llm-serving-70b p99_latency 120,000ms (threshold: 5,000ms)' — but error_rate=0.0%. Every in-flight completions request hangs with an open connection; no bytes are ever written to the response body. Your vLLM 0.5.1 cluster (2× AWS p4d.24xlarge, 8× A100-80GB per node, tensor_parallel_size=8) serves Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct at /v1/completions at 380 RPS for an enterprise code-assistant product. AWS performed a scheduled maintenance window at 22:30 UTC and upgraded the Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) driver from aws-efa-installer-1.30.0 → 1.31.0 on both instances without a change notification to the ML team.

kubectl logs llm-serving-0 -n inference --since=30m shows normal completions up to 22:44:03 UTC then silence. NCCL_DEBUG=INFO on rank 0:

  2026-05-15T22:44:07Z INFO  NCCL - ncclAllReduce: Using algorithm=TREE (size=268435456 bytes, threshold=67108864, afEFASupported=1)
  2026-05-15T22:44:09Z WARN  NCCL - rank 0: waiting for rank 5 in AllReduce [nflowsPerPeer=4, groupId=2] ... elapsed 2000ms
  2026-05-15T22:44:15Z WARN  NCCL - rank 0: waiting for rank 5 in AllReduce [nflowsPerPeer=4, groupId=2] ... elapsed 8000ms
  2026-05-15T22:44:29Z ERROR NCCL - ncclAllReduceKernel: timeout 22000ms. All 8 ranks blocked in double-binary-tree barrier. groupId=2, peerRank=5

nvidia-smi on the p4d instance: all 8 GPUs show Process=python3 (vllm worker), Memory=78432/81920MiB (96%), GPU-Util=0% — no kernel is executing. All 8 ranks are blocked in the NCCL double-binary-tree (DBT) all-reduce barrier.

Root cause: In EFA driver 1.31.0, the efa kernel module changed efa_use_device_rdma default from 0 → 1, enabling Device RDMA. NCCL 2.21.5 (bundled in PyTorch 2.3.0, required by vLLM 0.5.1) added a path: when efa_use_device_rdma=1 is detected, all-reduce operations above 64 MB switch from Ring → Double Binary Tree (DBT), which uses a new 2-step flow-control handshake across 4 NCCL channels. However, vLLM 0.5.1's async_llm_engine.py dispatches the all-reduce on a dedicated nccl_stream per rank that is not fully synchronized with that rank's attention CUDA stream before the DBT pre-send barrier check. On rank 5 (the 6th A100 in the 8-way TP group), the attention output is computed on cuda:5's default stream. The DBT pre-send barrier fires on nccl_stream5 before the default-stream attention kernel has produced its output. Rank 5 stalls at the barrier waiting for an event that is posted on the default stream — but the default stream is also waiting for nccl_stream5 to release a semaphore. Circular dependency: all 8 ranks deadlock. The RPC path continues to accept connections (HTTP layer is healthy) but all generation workers are frozen, so no response bytes ever complete. Business impact: 380 RPS × 22 minutes = 501,600 failed requests; 3 enterprise B2B customers trigger SLA credit calculations ($186K exposure); 74,000 inference jobs queue in the upstream pipeline.
items.exercise:
1. Distinguish deadlock from OOM: nvidia-smi shows 96% VRAM allocated with GPU-Util=0% on all 8 GPUs — an OOM crash would show a dead process (0 MiB) and a pod in CrashLoopBackOff. kubectl get pods -n inference shows Running (not CrashLoop). This is a live deadlock: processes are alive but stuck. Confirm NCCL hang: kubectl exec llm-serving-0 -n inference -- kill -3 <vllm-pid> (SIGQUIT triggers Python traceback): all worker threads blocked in ncclAllReduceKernel. Check NCCL log: kubectl logs llm-serving-0 -n inference | grep -E 'TREE|RING|timeout|rank 5' — see TREE algorithm selected at 22:44:07, timeout at 22:44:29.
2. Identify EFA driver as root cause: SSH to the p4d instance (or kubectl exec into the node-debug pod if using privileged DaemonSet): modinfo efa | grep efa_use_device_rdma — shows 'parm: efa_use_device_rdma:Enable device RDMA (default: 1 [changed in 1.31.0])'. Confirm version: apt list --installed 2>/dev/null | grep aws-efa-installer → 'aws-efa-installer/now 1.31.0'. Cross-reference with AWS EFA 1.31.0 release notes (aws.amazon.com/hpc/efa): 'efa_use_device_rdma now defaults to enabled.' This caused NCCL to switch from Ring → DBT for the 256-MB all-reduces vLLM performs during attention projection.
3. Immediate mitigation: Force NCCL to use Ring algorithm: kubectl set env deployment/llm-serving -n inference NCCL_ALGO=Ring NCCL_TREE_THRESHOLD=0. Rolling restart: kubectl rollout restart deployment/llm-serving -n inference (maxUnavailable=0, maxSurge=1). Each new pod starts with NCCL_ALGO=Ring — the DBT path is never taken, no stream synchronization issue. Deadlocked pods are terminated, releasing all 8 A100 CUDA contexts and draining hung HTTP connections. Monitor: kubectl rollout status deployment/llm-serving -n inference — first Ready pod in ~2 min.
4. Validate fix: After first new pod is Ready, run: for i in $(seq 20); do curl -s -w '%{http_code} %{time_total}\n' -X POST https://llm-prod.internal/v1/completions -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"model":"llama-3-1-70b-instruct","prompt":"Hello","max_tokens":20}' -o /dev/null; done — assert all HTTP 200, time_total < 4.0s. Confirm NCCL log: kubectl logs llm-serving-0 -n inference | grep 'algorithm=' — should show 'Using algorithm=RING' for all allReduce ops. Check Prometheus: vllm:generation_tokens_per_second should return to ~45,000 tok/s (baseline).
5. Long-term engine fix: vLLM async_llm_engine.py must synchronize each rank's attention output stream with the NCCL stream before the all-reduce call, regardless of the NCCL algorithm: add torch.cuda.current_stream(device).synchronize() before dispatcher._run_workers('execute_model', ...) in the TP path. File upstream vLLM GitHub issue: 'NCCL DBT pre-send barrier deadlock under EFA efa_use_device_rdma=1 (aws-efa-installer 1.31+, NCCL 2.21.5, PyTorch 2.3.0).' Track fix in vLLM 0.5.2+. Until upstream fix lands, keep NCCL_ALGO=Ring pinned.
6. Infrastructure hardening: (a) Pin EFA driver in the AWS Launch Template AMI: add UserData script assert: efa_version=$(modinfo efa | awk '/^version/{print $2}'); [[ "$efa_version" == '1.30.0' ]] || (echo 'EFA driver version mismatch — aborting AMI promotion' && exit 1). Block AMI promotion in CI until a 2-min NCCL health test passes: nccl_tests all_reduce_perf -b 64M -e 512M -f 2 -g 8 — a deadlock would cause this to hang and fail the job. (b) Add a deadlock-specific Prometheus alert: vllm:request_latency_p99 > 30000 AND vllm:error_rate < 0.01 for 3 min → PagerDuty P1 'LLM serving silent hang'. Standard error_rate alerts completely miss this class of failure — the symptom is high latency, not errors. (c) Add a NCCL liveness check to the Kubernetes readinessProbe: a 30s periodic exec probe runs python3 -c 'import torch.distributed as dist; dist.all_reduce(torch.zeros(1).cuda())' with a 10s timeout — pod is marked NotReady if the probe hangs, triggering a rolling replacement before user traffic is impacted.

items.drill: SageMaker Multi-Model Endpoint LRU Cache Thrashing — EU/US Time-Zone Traffic Shift Causes P99 Latency 28s
items.layer: ML Serving (AWS SageMaker MME / Python FastAPI / XGBoost / AWS)
items.scenario:
At 09:02 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'personalization-api-prod p99-latency-breach 28.4s (threshold: 2.0s)'. Your SageMaker Multi-Model Endpoint (MME) `personalization-mme-prod` (8x ml.g5.2xlarge instances, 24 GB GPU each, us-east-1) hosts 1,847 per-tenant XGBoost ranking models (avg 420 MB loaded in memory, 340 MB on disk in S3). SageMaker MME's built-in LRU cache holds ~55 models per instance (8 x 55 = 440 total warm slots across the fleet). EU tenant accounts (100-800) drive 51% of daily traffic; US accounts (1,000-1,847) dominate overnight.

At 09:00 UTC, the EU business day starts. EU models have been cold (evicted overnight) since 17:00 UTC the prior day. When EU traffic spikes from 0 to 18,000 req/min in 4 minutes:

  2026-05-29T09:00:14.221Z [INFO] sagemaker-mme: ModelLoadingStarted model=tenant_287_ranking_v3.tar.gz instance=personalization-mme-prod-ml-g5-2xlarge-0
  2026-05-29T09:00:14.889Z [INFO] sagemaker-mme: ModelEvicting model=tenant_1247_ranking_v2.tar.gz (LRU, last_access=2026-05-28T23:47:02Z)
  [... 1,847 eviction/load cycles across 8 instances over 6 minutes ...]

Each model load from S3 takes 4.8-7.2 seconds (340 MB download at ~50 MB/s + XGBoost deserialization). SageMaker MME load latency adds directly to inference latency from the caller's perspective -- the HTTP connection blocks until model loading completes. Download is single-threaded per instance. During the cache thrashing window, all 8 instances are simultaneously loading EU models while evicting US models.

CloudWatch metrics (09:00-09:09 UTC):
  SageMaker/ModelLoadingTime avg: 5.8s
  SageMaker/ModelUnloadingCount: 847 in first 6 minutes (normal: 0-2/hr)
  SageMaker/InvocationModelLoadingTime: 92% of all invocations contain model load overhead (normal: < 1%)
  SageMaker/InvocationsPerInstance P99: 4,200/min (near endpoint concurrency limit of 4,000)
  SageMaker/ModelCacheHitRate: 7% (normal: 98%)
  SageMaker/EndpointLatency P99: 28.4s (SLO: 2.0s)

Root cause: The MME LRU cache capacity (55 models x 8 instances = 440 warm slots) is insufficient for the 1,847-model fleet when two geographic traffic cohorts swap simultaneously. The daily EU/US boundary creates a predictable eviction storm that wasn't captured by any alarm. The FastAPI gateway (httpx client, timeout=10s) times out only 36% of requests; the other 64% wait the full 28.4s P99, holding open TCP connections and exhausting the upstream connection pool.

Business impact: personalization API P99 exceeds SLA for 142 EU enterprise tenants for 9 minutes. FastAPI httpx clients trigger static ranking fallback for top-percentile requests, degrading CTR 14.2% for EU tenants. $340K at-risk subscription revenue (SLA breach triggers credits). Two EU tenants open severity-1 tickets before automated alerts fire.
items.exercise:
1. Triage -- confirm model load overhead (not GPU OOM or CPU saturation): CloudWatch Metrics -> SageMaker/InvocationModelLoadingTime -- 92% of invocations at 09:01 UTC. Confirm cache miss: SageMaker/ModelCacheHitRate drops to 7%. Rule out instance saturation: CPUUtilization < 45%, MemoryUtilization < 62%. Check CloudTrail S3 GetObject latency: avg 4.9s for 340 MB tar.gz files -- normal for us-east-1 inter-AZ S3 throughput. This confirms bottleneck is sequential model cold-loading, not compute.
2. Immediate mitigation -- pre-warm EU models in parallel: Issue dummy inference requests for each EU tenant account to force model loading outside the production hot path. `aws sagemaker-runtime invoke-endpoint --endpoint-name personalization-mme-prod --target-model tenant_{id}_ranking_v3.tar.gz --body '{}' --content-type application/json /tmp/out.json`. Run in parallel across all 440 warm slots using asyncio: 440 models x 5.8s avg / 8 instances = ~40 concurrent loads per instance. Monitor: SageMaker/ModelCacheHitRate climbs back to >90% within ~5 min of warm-up completing.
3. Scale endpoint to increase cache capacity: `aws sagemaker update-endpoint-weights-and-capacities --endpoint-name personalization-mme-prod --desired-weights-and-capacities '[{"VariantName":"AllTraffic","DesiredInstanceCount":16}]'`. Doubling from 8->16 instances doubles warm-model slots to 880 -- enough to hold EU + US cohorts simultaneously (700 EU + 847 US = 1,547 < 880 with some eviction, or size to 34 instances for full coverage at 1,870 slots). New instances join in ~4 min (ml.g5.2xlarge start time).
4. Durable fix -- scheduled pre-warming via EventBridge + Lambda: (a) Rule 1: fires at 08:45 UTC daily -> Lambda warms EU tenant accounts 100-800 (700 models, ~5 min to warm across 8 instances at 40 concurrent loads/instance). (b) Rule 2: fires at 17:00 UTC daily -> warm US accounts 1,000-1,847. Lambda code: `asyncio.gather(*[invoke_endpoint(id) for id in tenant_ids], return_exceptions=True)` with MaxConcurrency=400 matching endpoint capacity. Add structured CloudWatch Logs for each warm-up invocation to audit pre-warm coverage.
5. Validate: Trigger manual EventBridge at 08:55 UTC in staging. Monitor SageMaker/ModelCacheHitRate -- expect >95% for EU tenant range within 5 min. Replay 1,000 EU tenant inference requests at 09:00 -- P99 should stay < 500ms (pure inference, no load overhead). Confirm ModelUnloadingCount stays < 50 during EU ramp-up.
6. Prevent: (a) Add CloudWatch alarm: ModelCacheHitRate < 80% for 2 consecutive 1-min periods -> PagerDuty P2 'MME cache hit rate degraded'. (b) Add alarm: ModelLoadingTime avg > 2s for 3 consecutive minutes -> PagerDuty P2. (c) Daily Lambda computes (peak_concurrent_tenants x avg_model_size) / (instances x instance_memory) and pages if ratio > 70%. (d) Evaluate SageMaker MME with NVIDIA Triton backend -- Triton's GPU model cache is 10x larger per instance, reducing cold-load frequency. (e) Long-term: migrate hot tenants to dedicated endpoints (InferenceGraph/KServe), keeping the MME only for the long tail of infrequent-access tenants.

items.drill: TGI Streaming Zombie Sessions — iOS Background TCP Suspension Exhausts KV Cache Pool
items.layer: ML Serving (text-generation-inference 2.1.0 / AWS SageMaker / Rust / iOS)
items.scenario:
At 09:14 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'chat-api-prod SageMakerInvocationErrors 89.3% (threshold: 5%)'. Your SageMaker real-time endpoint `mistral-chat-prod` (4× ml.g5.4xlarge, 1× NVIDIA A10G-24GB each, text-generation-inference 2.1.0) serves Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 for a mobile chat application. Model weights consume 14.1 GB; the paged-attention KV cache pool is allocated the remaining ~8.5 GB (~228 pages of 128 tokens each). At peak load each streaming session holds roughly 300 MB of KV cache pages.

At 05:00 UTC, iOS app version 4.2.0 shipped a background-fetch feature. When the iOS system suspends the app mid-session (e.g., incoming phone call, lock screen), it suspends the underlying TCP socket without sending a FIN or RST packet — the kernel TCP window simply shrinks to 0. Over 4 hours and 14 minutes, 2,847 such suspended sockets accumulate: users opened an SSE `/generate_stream` request and then backgrounded the app.

TGI's Rust streaming path in `router/src/server.rs` uses `axum::response::Sse::new(stream).keep_alive(KeepAliveConfig::default())`. `KeepAliveConfig::default()` sends a `:keepalive\n\n` SSE comment every 15s — but these comments queue into the Tokio `StreamWriter` internal buffer and are never flushed when the kernel TCP send window is 0. The underlying `poll_write` on `tokio_util::io::StreamWriter` returns `Poll::Pending` indefinitely (EAGAIN on the socket). Tokio parks the async task and never applies a timeout: no `tokio::time::timeout()` wraps the send path.

Each zombie Tokio task in `router/src/infer.rs` holds a `CacheEntry` — a live reference into the paged-attention KV cache pool — for the task's lifetime. The task is alive but blocked on TCP write and never returns its cache pages. By 09:14 UTC:
  2,847 zombie sessions × avg 2.97 MB KV cache = 8.46 GB consumed (entire 8.5 GB KV pool exhausted)

SageMaker CloudWatch at 09:14 UTC:
  GPUMemoryUtilization: 99.8% (23.95 GB / 24 GB)
  GPUUtilization: 2.3% (almost no generation running — only keepalive path alive)
  InvocationErrors: 89.3% (previously 0.0%)
  ModelLatency: 0ms (requests fail before reaching the model)

TGI container logs via CloudWatch Logs at 09:14:03 UTC:
  ERRO text_generation_router::infer - Scheduler error: InferError::Overloaded(\"Queue is full (max_waiting_tokens=20000, current=20000)\")
  ERRO text_generation_router::server - Generate stream error: Overloaded (\"Queue is full\")
  WARN text_generation_router::scheduler - Batch slots exhausted: all 228 KV-cache pages allocated (page_size=128tokens, kv_cache_total_pages=228, kv_cache_free_pages=0)
  ERRO text_generation_router::infer - Rejected request req_id=b9f82a3 reason=\"No free KV cache pages\"

`ss -tnp` on the container (via SageMaker SSM session) shows 2,847 ESTABLISHED connections with remote TCP receive window = 0 — the iOS suspended-socket signature. None appear in TGI's `tgi_request_count` or `tgi_request_success` Prometheus metrics because they are Tokio tasks blocked on TCP write, never completing generation. Standard metrics show 0 active requests, 0 errors, and healthy queue sizes — only GPUMemoryUtilization at 99.8% with near-zero GPU compute is anomalous.

Business impact: 89.3% of chat API requests fail for 31 minutes. 47,000 active mobile sessions receive HTTP 500. 4 enterprise B2B customers breach their 99.9% monthly uptime SLA ($156K penalty exposure). The on-call spends 19 minutes investigating the application layer before correlating the iOS 4.2.0 deploy with the GPU memory signature.
items.exercise:
1. Triage — confirm zombie session signature (not OOM or model bug): Check `tgi_queue_size` and `tgi_request_count{status='success'}` in CloudWatch — both near 0 despite 99.8% GPUMemoryUtilization. This 'high VRAM, low utilization, zero active requests' pattern uniquely identifies KV cache held by zombie tasks (a real OOM crash would show a dead process, 0 MiB allocated, and a pod in CrashLoopBackOff). Confirm via container SSM session: `ss -tnp | awk '$3==0{c++}END{print c}'` — count of ESTABLISHED sockets with zero TCP receive queue reveals the 2,847 suspended iOS connections. Rule out a model bug: a fresh container restart will immediately free all KV pages (all zombie Tokio tasks killed), confirming external TCP state — not model or routing logic — is holding the cache.
2. Immediate mitigation — cycle SageMaker containers to flush all zombie tasks: `aws sagemaker update-endpoint --endpoint-name mistral-chat-prod --endpoint-config-name mistral-chat-prod-config-20260601`. This terminates all 4 containers, kills all 2,847 zombie Tokio tasks, releases the full KV cache pool, and restarts TGI with a clean slate. Expect ~90s of full downtime. For a lower-impact option, add 2 clean instances via `update-endpoint-weights-and-capacities`, drain traffic from zombie instances to the new ones, then remove the zombie instances — achieving near-zero-downtime recovery at the cost of 4 extra minutes.
3. Immediate config fix — add `--client-idle-timeout` (TGI 2.2.0+): Deploy TGI 2.2.0 image (upgrade from 2.1.0) and add startup flag: `TGI_ARGS=--client-idle-timeout 30`. This flag terminates any streaming session where the TCP write buffer has been full for >30s, sending an SSE error event and releasing the KV cache pages. Deploy via SageMaker model update (update container environment variable) — zombie accumulation cannot restart until the next iOS 4.2.0 backgrounding event, giving a ~4h reprieve.
4. Root cause — Rust `poll_write` EAGAIN is valid backpressure; Tokio respects it indefinitely: In TGI 2.1.0, `router/src/server.rs:generate_stream_handler` uses `axum::response::Sse` backed by `tokio_util::io::StreamWriter`. When the iOS kernel TCP window reaches 0, every `StreamWriter::poll_write()` returns `Poll::Pending` (EAGAIN). Tokio's cooperative scheduler parks the task — a legitimate I/O-wait state. No `tokio::time::timeout()` wraps the write path, so the task waits forever. `KeepAliveConfig::default()` sends SSE `:keepalive` comments every 15s but only enqueues them into the Tokio send buffer — they do not probe the TCP socket reachability. Confirmed via `ss --processes --tcp state established` showing 2,847 connections all with `Send-Q > 0` (data queued, never draining).
5. Validate recovery: After container restart and TGI 2.2.0 deploy, run: `curl -N -H 'Content-Type: application/json' https://chat-api.internal/generate_stream -d '{"inputs":"ping","parameters":{"max_new_tokens":50}}'` — confirm SSE tokens stream normally. Monitor `GPUMemoryUtilization` — should return to ~62% (model weights + small active-request KV) within 2 minutes. Validate zombie protection: using `tc netem` simulate a TCP black-hole (`iptables -A OUTPUT -m statistic --mode nth --every 1 -j DROP` on the client side), connect an SSE stream, and assert TGI logs `client idle timeout after 30s` and releases the KV cache page — confirm `tgi_cache_utilization` drops within 35s.
6. Prevent: (a) Add a compound CloudWatch alarm: `GPUMemoryUtilization > 90% AND GPUUtilization < 10%` for 5 consecutive 1-minute periods → PagerDuty P1 'TGI zombie KV cache accumulation'. A normal high-load signature shows BOTH metrics elevated; this combination exclusively identifies zombie sessions. (b) Pin TGI to 2.2.0+ in the SageMaker model container ARN — add a CI check that rejects any model package with `tgi==2.1.x` in the container label. (c) Fix iOS app 4.2.1: register for `UIApplicationDidEnterBackgroundNotification` and explicitly cancel the URLSessionDataTask — `task.cancel()` sends a TCP FIN packet, allowing TGI to cleanly close the Tokio task and release KV pages within 100ms. (d) Add a monthly chaos canary: simulate 200 iOS TCP-suspension connections (using `iptables -I INPUT -s <client-ip> -j DROP`) and assert `GPUMemoryUtilization` stabilizes to baseline within `--client-idle-timeout + 10s`; alert if the KV cache does not recover, catching regression if the idle-timeout flag is accidentally removed.

items.drill: vLLM Disaggregated Prefill — NIXL KV Transfer Deadlock After KEDA Decode Scale-Down Halts Generation for 17 Minutes
items.layer: ML Serving (vLLM 0.6.3 Disaggregated Prefill / Python / AWS EKS / p5.48xlarge / NVIDIA H100 SXM5-80GB / NIXL / KEDA)
items.scenario:
At 15:18 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'llm-api-prod generation_tokens_per_second 0.0 (threshold: 15,000)' and 'llm-api-prod ttft_p99_ms 118,402ms (threshold: 3,000ms)' — but error_rate = 0.0%. All in-flight SSE connections hold open, HTTP 200 headers sent, zero tokens stream to clients.

Your vLLM 0.6.3 cluster runs in disaggregated prefill mode on AWS EKS (p5.48xlarge, 8× H100 SXM5-80GB per node):
  Prefill pool: 2 pods (tensor_parallel_size=4, handles prompt KV computation)
  Decode pool: 4 pods (tensor_parallel_size=4, generates tokens from transferred KV cache)
KV cache blocks are transferred prefill→decode via NIXL (NVIDIA’s inter-node RDMA transport over AWS EFA), using a pre-allocated 512-slot buffer pool per prefill pod.

At 15:00 UTC, a KEDA ScaledObject scaled the decode pool from 6→4 pods based on a weekend traffic forecast (40% RPS drop expected). Two decode pods terminated cleanly. At 15:08 UTC, an enterprise batch job began submitting long-context legal document analysis requests at 380 req/min with avg prompt_tokens = 28,000 (baseline: 1,200) — each request requiring 1,120 KV blocks vs baseline 48.

Prometheus at 15:18 UTC:
  vllm:generation_tokens_per_second = 0.0 (baseline: 38,000)
  vllm:ttft_p99_ms = 118,402
  vllm:kv_cache_usage_perc{role='decode',pod='decode-0'} = 99.9%
  vllm:kv_cache_usage_perc{role='decode',pod='decode-1'} = 99.8%
  vllm:kv_cache_usage_perc{role='decode',pod='decode-2'} = 99.9%
  vllm:kv_cache_usage_perc{role='decode',pod='decode-3'} = 99.7%
  vllm:disagg_nixl_transfer_queue_depth{pod='prefill-0'} = 512 (at maximum, saturated since 15:12 UTC)
  vllm:disagg_nixl_transfer_queue_depth{pod='prefill-1'} = 512 (at maximum)
  vllm:num_queued_requests{role='decode'} = 4,180
  vllm:request_success_total rate = 0.0/s  (error_rate = 0.0% because HTTP 200 was sent at accept-time before engine dispatch)

vLLM 0.6.3 disaggregated engine logs (prefill pod 0):
  2026-06-05T15:08:44Z WARN  vllm.disagg.nixl_connector - Decode free KV blocks = 0: cannot dispatch KV transfer for seq_id=74821 (kv_blocks_needed=1120, decode_free_blocks=0)
  2026-06-05T15:08:44Z WARN  vllm.disagg.scheduler - Prefill-to-decode handoff stalled: seq_id=74821 waiting 47,203ms for decode slot
  2026-06-05T15:12:09Z ERROR vllm.disagg.nixl_connector - NIXL buffer pool exhausted (pool_size=512, pending=512, all awaiting decode acknowledgment). New prefill scheduling suspended.
  2026-06-05T15:12:09Z ERROR vllm.engine.async_llm_engine - Engine halted: prefill scheduler (queue=612) and decode scheduler (queue=4180) both stalled. Circular dependency: prefill blocked on NIXL; NIXL blocked on decode ack; decode blocked on KV block availability.

Root cause: With 4 decode pods (vs 6 pre-scale), total decode KV capacity = 4 × 3.8M blocks = 15.2M. At 1,120 blocks per 28K-token request, decode saturates after ~13,500 concurrent sequences. The batch job’s 380 req/min intake rate exceeds drain rate, filling decode to 99.9%. Once decode free_blocks = 0, it stops acknowledging NIXL KV block transfers. The prefill pods’ 512-slot NIXL buffer pools fill within 9 minutes. vLLM 0.6.3’s disagg scheduler suspends all new prefill scheduling when NIXL buffers are exhausted — but already-accepted requests are also deadlocked (waiting for decode acks that will never come). Circular stall: prefill halted → NIXL full → decode won’t ack → decode KV full → existing sequences cannot complete. HTTP 200 was sent at Uvicorn accept-time, so error_rate = 0.0% throughout — all standard error-rate PagerDuty rules are completely blind to this class of failure.

Business impact: 18 enterprise customers receive no response tokens for 17 minutes. 312 document analysis jobs time out client-side. 4 accounts breach TTFT < 3s SLA. $420K estimated revenue impact (SLA penalty credits + client-side reprocessing compute costs).
items.exercise:
1. Triage — confirm disagg stall vs OOM crash: The '0% error rate + 0 generation tokens/sec + 99%+ decode KV usage' fingerprint is unique to disaggregated-prefill deadlock. An OOM crash would show CrashLoopBackOff with 0 MiB GPU allocated; a normal KV saturation in non-disagg mode would produce HTTP 429/503. Check vllm:disagg_nixl_transfer_queue_depth on both prefill pods — if at 512 (max), NIXL buffers are the bottleneck. Rule out EFA network failure: CloudWatch → EFA Metrics → EFANetworkRxDrops should be 0 (a true EFA failure would also crash the TP all-reduce, producing CrashLoop). Correlate timeline: KEDA scale-down at 15:00 + batch job start at 15:08 + NIXL saturation at 15:12 — the 9-minute gap between batch start and total stall is the NIXL buffer fill time.
2. Immediate mitigation — pause KEDA and scale decode back up: First pause KEDA to prevent it from fighting the manual scale: `kubectl annotate scaledobject vllm-decode-scaler keda.sh/paused=true -n inference`. Then: `kubectl scale deployment/vllm-decode -n inference --replicas=8`. New decode pods pull H100 weights from EFS model cache (~40s if warm, ~3 min if cold). Once decode free_blocks > 0, decode begins acking NIXL transfers — NIXL buffer pool drains, prefill scheduler unblocks, generation tokens/sec climbs from 0. Monitor: watch -n 5 'kubectl exec decode-0 -n inference -- curl -s localhost:8000/metrics | grep kv_cache_usage_perc' — expect drop from 99.9% to <75% within 3 min of new pods joining.
3. Rate-limit the batch job to stop re-saturation: The enterprise customer’s batch API key is injecting 28K-token requests at 380 req/min. Emergency: apply a per-key throttle at the Envoy rate_limit_service: reduce batch-tier to 40 req/min (1/10th current rate) until vllm:kv_cache_usage_perc{role='decode'} < 70%. Alternatively, return HTTP 429 with Retry-After: 120 for any request with prompt_tokens > 16384 until decode KV pressure drops. The goal is to let the decode queue drain and give new pods time to absorb existing in-flight requests.
4. Root cause — vLLM’s disagg scheduler lacks decode-side back-pressure: vLLM 0.6.3 accepts HTTP connections and returns 200 OK at the Uvicorn layer before dispatching to the disagg engine. There is no feedback path from decode KV saturation back to the HTTP accept layer. Fix: add a decode-capacity circuit breaker in the API gateway (Envoy ext_authz or a FastAPI middleware wrapper) that polls vllm:kv_cache_usage_perc{role='decode'} every 10s. When decode KV > 85%, return HTTP 429 with Retry-After to batch-tier callers while preserving capacity for interactive-tier callers (priority header routing). This breaks the silent-stall failure mode: clients receive a clear 429 rather than a hung SSE stream.
5. Validate: After decode scales to 8 pods and NIXL buffers drain: vllm:disagg_nixl_transfer_queue_depth drops from 512 → <20 on both prefill pods within 2 min. vllm:generation_tokens_per_second climbs from 0 → 20,000+ within 5 min (reduced from 38K baseline — still throttled by rate-limited batch job, expected). TTFT P99 drops from 118s → <5s. Confirm no NIXL re-saturation over the next 30 min with 40 req/min batch rate. Run a synthetic test: 20 concurrent 28K-token requests and assert all complete within 15s.
6. Prevent: (a) Add a KEDA external trigger on vllm:avg_prompt_tokens_per_request: if avg prompt length > 8,000 tokens, block all decode scale-down regardless of RPS (long-context requests consume 10-23× more KV blocks per slot). Configure alongside the existing RPS trigger with a `minReplicaCount` floor when the prompt-length trigger is active. (b) Add composite alert: (vllm:generation_tokens_per_second < 1,000) AND (vllm:num_running_requests > 50) AND (vllm:request_error_rate < 0.01) for 3 consecutive minutes → PagerDuty P1 'disagg serving stall'. Standard error-rate alerts will never fire for this class of failure. (c) Increase NIXL buffer pool size from 512 → 2,048 via `--disagg-nixl-buffer-pool-size 2048` — buys ~36 min before prefill scheduling halts (vs 9 min at 512), giving time for autoscaling to respond. (d) Enforce per-endpoint max_prompt_tokens at the API gateway: batch endpoints capped at 16,384 tokens, interactive at 8,192, long-context at a dedicated pool with pre-allocated decode capacity. (e) Monthly chaos test: scale decode from 8 → 4 pods with production-like batch traffic and assert the NIXL saturation alert fires within 5 min; validate the KEDA-pause + scale-up runbook resolves in <10 min.

items.drill: SGLang Radix Cache Miss Storm — Multi-Tenant System Prompt Rotation Causes 9× TTFB Spike
items.layer: ML Serving (SGLang 0.3.4 / AWS EKS / Python / NVIDIA A100-40GB)
items.scenario:
At 11:18 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'agent-api-prod ttfb_p99 16.8s (threshold: 2.0s)'. Your SGLang 0.3.4 cluster (6 pods, AWS EKS, g5.12xlarge, 4× A100-40GB each, us-east-1) serves a multi-tenant enterprise coding assistant. Each of 23 enterprise tenants has a unique system prompt (4K–14K tokens) embedded as a shared prefix for all their users' requests; SGLang's RadixAttention cache stores these prefix KV tensors in a radix tree indexed by token content hash.

At 11:12 UTC, your product team shipped a system prompt A/B test appending new safety guidelines to all 23 tenant prompts simultaneously. The rotation invalidated 100% of cached prefixes in a single deployment wave. Six minutes later:

  sglang:cache_hit_rate                    0.03  (baseline: 0.81)
  sglang:num_running_reqs                  847   (baseline: 120)
  sglang:num_waiting_reqs                  3,240 (baseline: 0)
  sglang:kv_cache_usage_perc               0.97  (baseline: 0.44)
  sglang:decode_throughput_tokens_per_sec  12.1  (baseline: 2,840)

With cache hit rate at 3%, every request must recompute KV tensors for the full 4K–14K token system prompt prefix from scratch. One tenant's 18K-token system prompt alone occupies 28% of the KV cache pool during recompute. The 6-pod cluster's A100 GPU compute is overwhelmed: 3,240 requests queue, each waiting 2–4 seconds for prefill. TTFB P99 spikes from 1.9s → 16.8s (9×). Root cause: the deployment pipeline had no SGLang cache warm-up step and rotated all 23 tenant prompts simultaneously rather than rolling.

Business impact: All 23 enterprise customers' coding assistants breach their SLA-backed P99 TTFB ≤ 3s. 847 user requests time out per minute; $180K/hr SLA penalty exposure.
items.exercise:
1. Diagnose: Correlate `sglang:cache_hit_rate` drop to 0.03 at 11:12 UTC with `kubectl rollout history deployment/sglang` — confirm the system prompt ConfigMap change deployed at 11:12:04. Access SGLang debug API: `GET /v1/debug/radix_cache` — confirm 0 nodes matching the new tenant prompt hashes. Check pod logs: `kubectl logs -l app=sglang | grep 'cache miss' | head -20` — should show `prefix_len=0 / 12288` for all requests, confirming cold radix tree.
2. Immediate mitigation — shadow warmup: Deploy a one-shot warmup Job that sends 1 representative request per tenant with the new system prompt to pre-populate radix cache entries:
  `kubectl create job cache-warmup --image=python:3.11 -- python -c "
import requests, os
for tenant_id, prompt in TENANT_PROMPTS.items():
    requests.post('http://sglang-service/v1/chat/completions',
        json={'messages': [{'role': 'system', 'content': prompt},
                           {'role': 'user', 'content': 'warm'}]})
"`
After the 23 warmup requests complete (~90s), `sglang:cache_hit_rate` should recover to >0.75 as real user traffic hits the warmed prefixes.
3. Rate-limit incoming traffic: Temporarily halve `max_running_reqs` from 200 → 100 via SGLang's `--max-running-reqs 100` (rolling restart, 1 pod at a time) to prevent KV cache OOM under the heavy concurrent prefill load. This sheds 50% of overflow load while warmup completes.
4. Root fix — pre-deployment cache warm-up gate in ArgoCD: Add an AnalysisTemplate step that fires the warmup job against the canary pod and blocks promotion until `sglang:cache_hit_rate` exceeds 0.70:
```yaml
metrics:
- name: sglang-cache-warmup
  provider:
    prometheus:
      query: min(sglang_cache_hit_rate{pod=~"sglang-canary.*"})
  successCondition: result[0] >= 0.70
  interval: 30s
  count: 3
  failureLimit: 1
```
This gate fires warmup against a single canary replica and confirms cache population before shifting any user traffic.
5. Validate: Post-warmup: `sglang:cache_hit_rate` ≥ 0.78. `sglang:num_waiting_reqs` drains from 3,240 → 0 within 4 min of warmup completion. TTFB P99 returns to 1.9s. Confirm per-tenant cache hit: `GET /v1/debug/radix_cache?prefix=<tenant_hash>` returns non-empty entries for all 23 tenants.
6. Prevent: (a) Stagger system prompt rotations: rotate 1–2 tenants per minute (deploy via Helm value overrides with a rate-limited rollout), limiting cold-cache surface to ≤10% of traffic at any time. (b) Add Prometheus alert: `sglang:cache_hit_rate < 0.40 for 2m` → PagerDuty P2. (c) Use SGLang's `--kv-cache-retention-seconds` for versioned prefix segments so prompts unchanged across a rotation keep their cache entries. (d) Maintain a synthetic canary that sends one request per tenant every 60s; a sudden P95 TTFB spike on all canaries simultaneously is a reliable pre-deploy rotation smoke test.

items.drill: TensorRT-LLM FP8 cuBLAS GEMM Silent NaN Cascade — GKE Node Pool CUDA 12.6 Upgrade Produces Garbled Outputs at 0% Error Rate
items.layer: ML Serving (TensorRT-LLM 0.10.0 / NVIDIA H100 SXM5-80GB / GCP GKE Autopilot / cuBLAS 12.6 / Python)
items.scenario:
At 14:47 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'llm-api-prod auto-eval-quality-monitor avg_score 3.8/10 (threshold: 7.0/10) for 52 minutes'. Your TensorRT-LLM 0.10.0 cluster (6 pods on GCP GKE Autopilot, NVIDIA a3-highgpu-8g nodes, 8× H100 SXM5-80GB per node) serves Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 in FP8 quantization (--quantization fp8, block_size=128) at 940 RPS for an enterprise document analysis product. The quality monitor is an offline LLM judge (Gemini 1.5 Pro) that samples 200 random responses per 5-minute window and scores them for coherence.

At 13:52 UTC, a GKE node pool rolling upgrade (gpu-a3-pool, n=6) completed, moving from container image nvcr.io/nvidia/tensorrt:24.04-py3 (CUDA 12.4.1, cuBLAS 12.4.5.8) to nvcr.io/nvidia/tensorrt:24.06-py3 (CUDA 12.6.0, cuBLAS 12.6.1.4). The upgrade completed with no pod restarts — GKE Autopilot seamlessly migrated pods onto the new node images.

Prometheus at 14:47 UTC:
  trt_llm:request_success_rate = 1.000  (0.0% error rate)
  trt_llm:request_latency_p99_ms = 1,847  (baseline: 1,900ms — normal)
  trt_llm:gpu_memory_used_bytes{pod='trt-llm-0'}: 71.2 GB / 80 GB (baseline: 70.8 GB — normal)
  trt_llm:tokens_per_second = 41,200  (baseline: 41,800 — normal)
  nvidia_smi_gpu_utilization_percent: 91% all 6 nodes  (normal)
  llm_quality_judge:avg_score_5m: 8.3 → 3.8 (step change at 13:55 UTC)

Sample API responses after 13:55 UTC (observed via quality monitor logs):
  Input: 'Summarize the following legal clause regarding liability in 3 sentences:...'
  Expected: 'The clause limits company liability to...'
  Actual: '! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! !'

  Input: 'What are the key financial risks identified in Q3 earnings?'
  Actual: 'the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the the'

HTTP responses return 200 OK with valid JSON. TensorRT-LLM container logs show no errors:
  [TRT-LLM] [INFO] Batch 947231: 12 sequences completed. avg_tokens=47.3 latency=1831ms
  [TRT-LLM] [INFO] Batch 947232: 8 sequences completed. avg_tokens=51.1 latency=1879ms

Root cause: TensorRT-LLM 0.10.0 uses cuBLAS GemmEx (CUBLASLT_MATMUL_DESC_TRANSA + CUBLASLT_EPILOGUE_DEFAULT) with FP8 scaling factors for attention projection layers (q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj). In cuBLAS 12.6.1.4, the FP8 GemmEx kernel for H100 SXM5 changed its default algorithm selection (CUBLASLT_ALGO_ID_AUTO) to prefer CUBLASLT_MATMUL_ALGO_TILING_64_64 over the prior CUBLASLT_MATMUL_ALGO_TILING_128_128. The 64×64 tiling variant requires the FP8 descaling factor buffer to be 64-byte aligned, while TRT-LLM 0.10.0 allocates per-layer scaling tensors using the 128-byte alignment of the FP8 quantization block_size. For batch sizes where sequence_length × 7 is not divisible by 128 (the common case for variable-length inference), the scaling buffer pointer passed to GemmEx falls 64 bytes short of the 64-byte alignment requirement. cuBLAS 12.6 does not validate pointer alignment before kernel launch — the misaligned read silently fetches adjacent memory for the FP8 scaling factor, producing 0.0 (null memory region) instead of the correct per-block scale. FP8 dequantization with scaling factor 0.0 → NaN propagation through the attention layer MLP. After softmax on NaN logits, all output probabilities are uniform 1/vocab_size; greedy sampling selects the lowest-frequency token in the vocabulary repeatedly. Token ID 0 (the padding/BOS token, represented as '!' in the Mistral tokenizer) repeats indefinitely until max_new_tokens is reached.

Business impact: 52-minute window of garbled responses at 0% error rate. 49,200 document analysis API calls returned garbage. 8 enterprise customers ingested garbled outputs into their production RAG knowledge bases — requiring manual knowledge base audit and rollback. $340K SLA penalty exposure. RAG knowledge base contamination is difficult to bound — some customers have no version history of their vector DB contents.
items.exercise:
1. Triage — correlate quality drop with infrastructure change, not application change: check `kubectl rollout history deployment/mistral-trt -n inference` — last rollout was 4 days ago (no recent app change). Check GKE node pool upgrade log: `gcloud container operations list --filter='status=DONE AND startTime>2026-06-15T13:00:00Z' --format='value(name,startTime,endTime,operationType)'` — reveals gpu-a3-pool upgrade completed at 13:52 UTC, 3 minutes before quality score step-change at 13:55 UTC. Confirm CUDA version on running node: `kubectl debug node/gke-prod-a3-0 -it --image=ubuntu -- bash -c 'nvidia-smi && cat /usr/local/cuda/version.txt'` — shows CUDA 12.6.0. On a pre-upgrade node image (from a snapshot): CUDA 12.4.1. Confirm the token pattern: `kubectl exec trt-llm-0 -n inference -- python3 -c "import requests, json; r=requests.post('http://localhost:8000/v1/completions', json={'prompt':'Explain quantum computing in two sentences.','max_tokens':50}); print(r.json()['choices'][0]['text'])"` — expect repeating '! ! !' or 'the the the'.
2. Immediate mitigation — disable FP8 quantization (fall back to FP16): Update the TensorRT-LLM serving ConfigMap to remove `--quantization fp8` and set `--dtype float16`. Rebuild the TRT-LLM engine: `kubectl exec trt-llm-0 -n inference -- python3 /opt/trt-llm/build.py --model_dir /models/mistral-7b-instruct-v0.3 --dtype float16 --output_dir /engines/mistral-fp16 --max_batch_size 32`. Rolling restart pods to serve the FP16 engine (keep 2 pods on FP8 for post-incident diagnosis): `kubectl rollout restart deployment/mistral-trt -n inference --patch '{"spec":{"strategy":{"rollingUpdate":{"maxUnavailable":4}}}}'`. Memory impact: FP16 increases model memory from 8.1 GB → 14.2 GB per replica — still within 80 GB H100 headroom. Throughput impact: -18% (FP16 GEMM vs FP8 tensor cores). Verify: after 2 restarted pods, run 20 test prompts — all should return coherent text.
3. Confirm cuBLAS version as root cause: On the 2 remaining FP8 pods, run a minimal reproducer: `kubectl exec trt-llm-1 -n inference -- python3 -c "import ctypes; lib=ctypes.CDLL('libcublasLt.so.12'); print(lib.cublasLtGetVersion())"` — should return 120601004 (12.6.1.4). Roll back one node to the old image for A/B comparison: `gcloud container node-pools update gpu-a3-pool --node-version 1.29.x --image-type CUSTOM --image nvcr.io/nvidia/tensorrt:24.04-py3` on a single node, cordon all others, reschedule the FP8 pod to the old-image node, and run the same reproducer — expect coherent output, confirming the cuBLAS 12.6 regression.
4. Pin cuBLAS 12.4 in the production container (durable fix): Update the Dockerfile to explicitly pin cuBLAS version and prevent automatic resolution to 12.6: `FROM nvcr.io/nvidia/tensorrt:24.06-py3` + `RUN pip install 'nvidia-cublas-cu12==12.4.5.8' --force-reinstall && ldconfig`. Add CI pinning assertion: `RUN python3 -c "import ctypes; v=ctypes.CDLL('libcublasLt.so.12').cublasLtGetVersion(); assert v == 120405008, f'cuBLAS version {v} != 12.4.5.8 (pinned)'`. Alternatively, if TRT-LLM 0.10.1+ releases a fix (upstream bug report filed: github.com/NVIDIA/TensorRT-LLM/issues/XXXX), upgrade TRT-LLM and validate the FP8 scaling alignment fix via the official FP8 correctness test suite: `python3 tests/quantization/test_fp8_gemm_alignment.py --device h100 --batch_sizes 1,4,7,13,31`.
5. Validate: After cuBLAS pin, run 1,000 FP8 inference requests across all variable batch sizes (1, 4, 7, 11, 13, 31): `for bs in 1 4 7 11 13 31; do python3 eval_batch.py --batch_size $bs --num_batches 20 --model mistral-7b-instruct-v0.3 --engine /engines/mistral-fp8; done` — assert zero repeating-token sequences and quality score ≥ 7.8 (measured by Gemini judge).
