5. Validate: Deploy the fix (the paused rollout: kubectl rollout resume deployment/order-events-consumer). Monitor during rolling update: Cloud Monitoring pubsub/subscription/ack_message_count should remain flat at ~14,000/min with no drop per pod termination. pubsub/subscription/undelivered_message_count should stay below 5K throughout. Cloud Logging: zero 'task dropped before ack' log lines during subsequent rollouts. Run duplicate check query again post-rollout — expect zero duplicate order IDs.
6. Prevent: (a) Add a Tokio graceful-shutdown integration test using the Pub/Sub emulator (pubsub-emulator Docker image): publish 500 messages, send SIGTERM to the consumer process via nix::sys::signal::kill() mid-processing, assert all 500 ackIds appear in the emulator's acknowledged list — this test would have caught the bug in CI. (b) Add a Clippy lint (custom lint via dylint) that flags bare tokio::spawn() calls inside functions named *handler* or *subscriber* — enforces JoinSet usage for tracked async tasks in consumer code. (c) Add Cloud Monitoring alert: rate(pubsub_subscription_ack_message_count[2m]) < 5000 AND rate(pubsub_subscription_pull_message_count[2m]) > 5000 → PagerDuty P1 'consumer ack stall'. This fires within 2 minutes of the next redelivery storm vs the 12-minute gap in this incident. (d) Document the structured-concurrency rule in CONTRIBUTING.md: 'All async tasks that perform external side effects (acks, writes, RPC calls) MUST be tracked in a JoinSet or equivalent — no bare tokio::spawn() for work that must complete before shutdown.'

items.drill: Elasticsearch Disk Flood Stage — force_merge Segment Merge Overhead Triggers Mass Index Read-Only Lockout
items.layer: Distributed Systems (Elasticsearch 8.12.2 / AWS ECS / Java Spring Boot)
items.scenario:
At 07:33 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'event-ingestion-service-prod error_rate 100% (threshold: 2%)'. Your Elasticsearch 8.12.2 cluster (6 data nodes, AWS ECS, r6g.4xlarge, 500GB gp3 EBS each, us-east-1) stores 2.1TB of event analytics data across 48 daily indices (event-logs-YYYY.MM.DD, 3 primary shards × 1 replica each). Disk per-node at baseline: 71% (355GB/500GB).

At 07:15 UTC, an automated daily maintenance job force-merges 6 aging indices (30 days old) to reclaim segment file handles:

  POST /event-logs-2026.04.*/_forcemerge?max_num_segments=1

A force_merge with max_num_segments=1 temporarily writes the new merged segment alongside the existing segments before deleting the originals. The 6 indices being merged hold 280GB. Merge overhead adds ~40% of the source size in temporary disk:
  355GB (baseline) + 112GB (merge temporaries) = 467GB / 500GB = 93.4% — approaching flood stage.

At 07:26:33 UTC, JVM heap pressure on data-node-4 (28GB G1 heap) triggers back-to-back Full GC pauses, deferring segment file descriptor release for 4.2s:

  [2026-05-20T07:26:33.847+0000][gc] GC(9284) Pause Full (G1 Evacuation Pause) 25188M->23941M(28672M) 4.201s
  [2026-05-20T07:26:42.019+0000][gc] GC(9285) Pause Full (System.gc()) 26001M->24104M(28672M) 3.971s

The GC-deferred release spikes node-4 disk to 95.7%, crossing Elasticsearch's cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.flood_stage (default 95%). Elasticsearch logs at 07:26:44 UTC:

  WARN  [o.e.c.r.a.DiskThresholdMonitor] [data-node-4] flood stage disk watermark [95%] exceeded on [node-4][/data/nodes/0] free: 17.8gb[3.6%], all indices on this node will be marked read-only
  INFO  [o.e.c.m.MetadataIndexStateService] [master-1] adding block BLOCK_WRITES to index [event-logs-2026.05.20], cause [flood stage disk watermark exceeded on node data-node-4]
  [47 additional identical lines — one per index cluster-wide]

ES 8.x flood-stage behavior: when ANY node crosses flood stage, Elasticsearch applies index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete=true to ALL indices cluster-wide (not just those on the affected node). All writes fail immediately. Spring Boot application logs:

  2026-05-20T07:33:02.441Z ERROR ElasticsearchBulkProcessor - BulkItemResponse failure:
    ClusterBlockException[blocked by: [TOO_MANY_REQUESTS/12/disk usage exceeded flood-stage watermark, index has read-only-allow-delete block];]
    index=event-logs-2026.05.20 shard=2 documentCount=500 bulkSizeBytes=14882
    [847 requests/sec, all returning HTTP 403]
  2026-05-20T07:33:03.891Z WARN  RetryHandler - Retrying bulk request attempt 2/3 (max retries: 3, drop after)

The force_merge completes at 07:28:51 UTC. Old segments deleted. Disk drops to 73.2% — well below flood stage. But Elasticsearch does NOT automatically remove read_only_allow_delete blocks after disk recovers. The block persists until explicitly cleared via the settings API.

Grafana Elasticsearch cluster health dashboard shows: cluster_status=GREEN, unassigned_shards=0, disk_used_percent=73.2%. The write block is invisible to standard ES health metrics. On-call spends 18 minutes investigating the Spring Boot application layer before checking index blocks. During that time, 304,920 events (847/sec × 360s) are silently dropped after 3 retries with no dead-letter recovery path. Three downstream ML models serving personalization features read stale data for 14 minutes post-recovery. Business impact: 304,920 lost behavioral events, 3 ML feature pipelines degraded, estimated $110K revenue from degraded personalization CTR during the blackout window.
items.exercise:
1. Triage — check index blocks directly (not cluster health): GET /_all/_settings/index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete — returns all indices with block set to true. If this returns a large number of indices, it confirms flood-stage lockout. Standard health endpoints (GET /_cluster/health, Kibana index management) will show GREEN/healthy — the write block is invisible to them. Cross-reference: search CloudWatch Logs for /aws/ecs/elasticsearch-cluster with filter 'flood stage disk watermark exceeded' — confirms the event at 07:26:44 UTC on data-node-4. Check current disk usage: GET /_cat/nodes?v&h=name,disk.used_percent,disk.avail — should show 73.2% (recovered). This proves the flood stage was transient.
2. Immediate fix — clear the read_only_allow_delete block: PUT /_all/_settings {"index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete": null}. The null value removes the setting entirely. This is zero-downtime — no restart, no shard movement. Verify: GET /_all/_settings/index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete should return an empty object (no indices with the block). Application writes resume within 1–2 seconds as the Spring Boot BulkProcessor retry queue drains. Monitor event-ingestion-service error rate in CloudWatch — should return to 0% within 30s.
3. Root cause — force_merge merge overhead + GC interaction: The automated maintenance job ran force_merge at 07:15 on 280GB of indices with max_num_segments=1. The merge generates temporary disk usage equal to 30–50% of source data while old and new segments coexist. On data-node-4, the G1GC Full GC pause (4.2s) deferred OS-level segment file release for ~4s, pushing disk utilization the final 1.7% past flood stage. Run: GET /_tasks?detailed=true&actions=*forcemerge to see active merge tasks; check CloudWatch Logs for GC pause events preceding the flood-stage alert.
4. Recover lost events: 304,920 dropped events have no automatic recovery — the ingestion service discards after 3 retries. Add a dead-letter SQS queue as an emergency DLQ: when ES bulk returns 403, serialize raw events to SQS dead-letter-events-prod for later replay. Immediately check whether events from the 07:26–07:33 window still exist in the upstream Kinesis stream (7-day retention): aws kinesis get-shard-iterator --stream-name event-stream-prod --shard-iterator-type AT_TIMESTAMP --timestamp '2026-05-20T07:26:00Z' — iterate and replay to ES now that the block is cleared.
5. Fix the merge strategy: max_num_segments=1 maximizes merge overhead. Switch to max_num_segments=5 (reduces temporary disk overhead to ~10% of source size). Better: for 30-day-old indices, call DELETE /event-logs-2026.04.* instead of force_merge — this frees disk entirely with zero overhead. If merging is required for performance (read optimization), run it node-by-node with a per-node disk check first: GET /_cat/nodes?v&h=name,disk.used_percent — abort the merge if any node exceeds 80%. Add to the maintenance job: assert all nodes < 80% disk before calling _forcemerge.
6. Prevent: (a) Automated block remediation — deploy a Lambda that runs every 2 minutes: (1) calls GET /_all/_settings/index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete, (2) if any blocks found AND GET /_cat/nodes shows all disk < 85%, automatically calls PUT /_all/_settings {"index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete": null} and pages PagerDuty P2 'ES flood-stage auto-cleared'. This reduces the 18-minute human discovery delay to <2 minutes. (b) Synthetic write health check: CloudWatch Events rule triggers a Lambda every 60s to PUT a test doc to health-check-write-index and assert HTTP 200 (not 403). This catches any write block within 60s regardless of cause — invisible to all standard ES health metrics. (c) Add CloudWatch alarm on custom metric es_disk_used_percent_max > 85% for any node (pushed from a Lambda polling GET /_cat/nodes every minute) → PagerDuty P2 'ES disk high-watermark approaching flood stage'. At 73% baseline this fires with 12% headroom before flood stage — enough time to pause maintenance jobs. (d) Set cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.high: '88%' and cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.flood_stage: '95%' explicitly in elasticsearch.yml — prevents surprises from default changes across upgrades. Update these values whenever baseline disk usage increases above 75%.

items.drill: SQS Visibility Timeout Race — Lambda Duration Spike Causes Duplicate Payment Charges During Flash Sale
items.layer: Distributed Systems (AWS SQS / Lambda Node.js 20.x / DynamoDB / Stripe API)
items.scenario:
At 19:31 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'payment-service-prod Stripe duplicate_charge_detected 43/min (threshold: 0)'. Your payment processing pipeline (AWS Lambda Node.js 20.x, memory=1,536MB, timeout=60s) is triggered by SQS queue payment-processing-prod (standard queue, VisibilityTimeout=30s, batch size=1). A flash sale at 19:15 UTC drove Lambda ConcurrentExecutions from 5 → 52 within 6 minutes. Lambda Duration baseline is 17s per order (Stripe API: 280ms; SageMaker fraud endpoint ml-fraud-score-prod: 420ms; DynamoDB reads/writes: 180ms; overhead/logging/config: ~16s). During the sale two downstream dependencies degrade simultaneously:
  — Stripe payment API: 280ms → 1.2s average (load-related; Stripe declares no incident)
  — SageMaker fraud endpoint: 420ms → 9.8s P99 (ml-fraud-score-prod's 2-instance concurrency limit saturated by 52 simultaneous Lambda invocations)

Combined, Lambda Duration P99 rises from 17,100ms → 31,240ms, exceeding VisibilityTimeout=30,000ms.

CloudWatch Logs Insights (`filter @message like /ReceiveCount=2/`) on /aws/lambda/payment-processor-prod:

  2026-05-19T19:27:14.182Z WARN  PaymentHandler - SQS re-delivery: MessageId=f3a9b1c2-8e7d-4f2e-9b3c-6d4e5f7a8b9c OrderId=ORD-74821 ReceiveCount=2
  [6,218 identical warnings between 19:24–19:51 UTC]
  2026-05-19T19:27:14.331Z INFO  StripeService - charge.create orderId=ORD-74821 chargeId=ch_3RLmQpHx9Ik2wZ4R0pXbNyJ5 amount=18700 currency=usd status=succeeded (DUPLICATE)
  2026-05-19T19:27:14.898Z ERROR IdempotencyGuard - ConditionalCheckFailedException orderId=ORD-74821 — existing chargeId=ch_3RLmQpHx9Ik2wZ4R0pXbNyJ4 vs new ch_3RLmQpHx9Ik2wZ4R0pXbNyJ5. Stripe charge NOT rolled back.

CloudWatch metrics (19:24–19:51 UTC):
  Lambda/Duration P99: 31,240ms (baseline: 17,100ms)
  Lambda/ConcurrentExecutions: 52
  Lambda/Throttles: 0 (no reserved concurrency cap configured)
  SQS VisibilityTimeout (configured): 30s
  DynamoDB/ConditionalCheckFailedRequests on payment_idempotency: 6,218
  Stripe Dashboard: 847 unique orders confirmed with duplicate charge.succeeded events

Root cause — two compounding bugs:
(1) SQS VisibilityTimeout=30s was set at queue creation in 2024 when Lambda Duration baseline was 8s. As payment logic grew (fraud model, feature enrichment, audit logging), Duration reached 17s — still within 30s until the first multi-dependency degradation event. No alarm monitored Duration relative to VisibilityTimeout.
(2) Idempotency guard fires in the wrong order: the Lambda calls stripe.charges.create() BEFORE writing the DynamoDB idempotency record. A re-delivered Lambda (starting at T=30s) reads DynamoDB at T=30.1s — the original Lambda is mid-execution between its Stripe call (T=25s) and its DynamoDB write (T=26.3s). No idempotency record exists yet → re-delivery passes the check → Stripe is called → duplicate charge committed. When the re-delivered Lambda then tries its own DynamoDB PutItem, the ConditionalCheckFailedException is caught and logged, but the handler returns 200 OK without calling stripe.refunds.create() to reverse the already-committed duplicate charge.

Business impact: 847 customers double-charged ($187 average = $158,389 total). Customer support +1,200 inbound tickets within 20 minutes. PCI-DSS incident notification triggered. Stripe chargeback exposure: $158,389 × 2.1% estimated rate = $3,326.
items.exercise:
1. Triage: Confirm SQS re-delivery (not Lambda crash-loop) as root cause. In Logs Insights on /aws/lambda/payment-processor-prod: `fields @timestamp, @message | filter @message like /ReceiveCount=2/ | stats count() by bin(1min)` — confirm 6,218 events between 19:24–19:51 UTC. Verify Duration P99 via CloudWatch Metrics → AWS/Lambda → Duration → p99 = 31,240ms. Confirm VisibilityTimeout: `aws sqs get-queue-attributes --queue-url https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789012/payment-processing-prod --attribute-names VisibilityTimeout` → '30'. Open X-Ray Service Map (19:15–19:52 UTC): identify ml-fraud-score-prod P99 = 9.8s and Stripe API P99 = 1.2s as the two dependencies driving Duration P99 above the 30s threshold. Confirm the idempotency ordering bug: grep handler code for the Stripe call position relative to the DynamoDB PutItem — Stripe appears on line 47, DynamoDB write on line 68, meaning 1.3s of vulnerability window exists between them where a re-delivery succeeds.
2. Immediate mitigation: (a) Increase VisibilityTimeout to 120s: `aws sqs set-queue-attributes --queue-url https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789012/payment-processing-prod --attributes '{"VisibilityTimeout":"120"}'`. All Lambdas complete within 60s (Lambda timeout ceiling); 120s provides a 2× buffer against any downstream degradation. Blocks all new duplicate charges immediately. (b) Throttle Lambda concurrency to relieve SageMaker endpoint pressure: `aws lambda put-function-concurrency --function-name payment-processor-prod --reserved-concurrent-executions 10`. At 10 concurrent Lambdas × 1 SageMaker call each = 10 concurrent fraud inferences — within the endpoint's provisioned throughput. SageMaker P99 should recover from 9.8s to <1s within 90s. (c) Begin batch-refunding 847 duplicates: `stripe charges list --created[gte]=1747683900 --created[lte]=1747686660 | jq '.data[] | select(.metadata.is_redelivery=="true") | .id'` → `stripe refunds create --charge=<id> --reason=duplicate`. Log all refund IDs in the P1 incident ticket for compliance.
3. Fix idempotency order (root-cause fix): In src/handlers/payment.js, restructure processing: (a) FIRST: DynamoDB PutItem with ConditionExpression='attribute_not_exists(orderId)' and TTL attribute set to Math.floor(Date.now()/1000)+3600. On ConditionalCheckFailedException → this orderId is already in-flight or completed; return HTTP 200 immediately without any Stripe call. (b) ONLY after PutItem succeeds: call `await stripe.charges.create(payload, {idempotencyKey: 'order-'+orderId})`. (c) On Stripe error: DeleteItem the DynamoDB record to allow a clean retry. This structure makes the DynamoDB PutItem a distributed mutex — only one Lambda can win the conditional write and proceed to Stripe regardless of SQS re-delivery timing or concurrency. Deploy via Lambda versioning blue/green: `aws lambda publish-version --function-name payment-processor-prod` → update alias `stable` to new version → SQS Event Source Mapping already points to alias.
4. Add Stripe-native idempotency as defense-in-depth: Pass `idempotencyKey` on every Stripe call: `await stripe.charges.create({amount, currency, source}, {idempotencyKey: 'order-'+orderId+'-v1'})`. Stripe deduplicates charges sharing the same key within 24h and returns the original charge object on retry with no new payment created. This backstop operates independently of Lambda/DynamoDB state and survives any future idempotency regressions.
5. Validate: Deploy fixed handler. Load-test with 100 orders and injected delays (mock Stripe SDK at 1.3s, mock SageMaker SDK at 10s): (a) Logs Insights — `filter @message like /ReceiveCount=2/` returns 0 results with VisibilityTimeout=120s. (b) Force-inject a re-delivery scenario in staging: reduce VisibilityTimeout to 5s, process 20 orders with 8s artificial delay — assert DynamoDB ConditionalCheckFailedRequests=20 (one per re-delivery) and Stripe duplicate charge count=0. (c) Production validation: `stripe charges list --created[gte]=<deploy_epoch> | jq '[.data | group_by(.metadata.order_id)[] | select(length > 1)] | length'` must return 0.
6. Prevent: (a) Add CloudWatch alarm: AWS/Lambda Duration P99 > (SQS VisibilityTimeout × 0.75) for 3 consecutive 1-minute periods → PagerDuty P2 'payment Lambda P99 approaching SQS VisibilityTimeout — duplicate charge risk'. At VisibilityTimeout=120s this fires when P99 > 90s, leaving 30s headroom before re-queuing begins. Re-evaluate the threshold whenever Lambda code or queue attributes change — add to service runbook. (b) Enforce Lambda scaling cap via Event Source Mapping: `aws lambda update-event-source-mapping --uuid <esm-uuid> --scaling-config '{"MaximumConcurrency": 12}'` — caps Lambda instances at the ESM layer regardless of SQS depth, preventing SageMaker endpoint saturation during demand spikes. (c) Add SQS Dead Letter Queue (maxReceiveCount=3): any message re-delivered 3+ times moves to payment-processing-dlq and pages PagerDuty P2 — ensures no order is silently dropped or processed in an infinite loop. (d) Add SageMaker endpoint scheduled scaling: Application Auto Scaling policy scaling ml-fraud-score-prod from 2 to 8 instances when InvocationsPerInstance > 8 for 60s; scheduled scale-out to 4 instances Mon–Fri 09:00–22:00 UTC to anticipate business-hours surges. (e) Require idempotency integration test in CI: LocalStack-based test sends 5 identical SQS messages for the same orderId (simulating 5 concurrent re-deliveries), asserts exactly 1 Stripe charge is created and 4 ConditionalCheckFailedRequests are recorded — catches incorrect idempotency ordering on every code change to the payment handler.

items.drill: RabbitMQ Quorum Queue Memory Alarm — Celery worker_prefetch_multiplier Surge Triggers OOM Wave and Blocks All Pika Producers on GKE
items.layer: Distributed Systems (RabbitMQ 3.13.1 / GKE 1.29 / Python 3.12 / Celery 5.4 / Pika 1.3.2)
items.scenario:
At 16:44 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'ml-inference-coordinator-prod error_rate 100% (threshold: 2%)' simultaneously with 'rabbitmq-prod memory-alarm ACTIVE'. Your RabbitMQ 3.13.1 cluster (3-node StatefulSet, GKE n2-highmem-8, 8 vCPU / 64 GB RAM, us-central1) coordinates a batch ML inference pipeline. Queue `ml-inference-tasks` is a quorum queue with `x-max-length=500000`. Consumer: 24 Celery 5.4 worker pods (`task_acks_late=True`). At 16:30 UTC, a DevOps engineer bumped `CELERYD_PREFETCH_MULTIPLIER` from 100 → 2000 for a 'performance tuning' sprint. Each worker now holds 2,000 unacked tasks in its internal prefetch buffer (avg payload 8.2 KB per task). Total unacked in-memory set: 24 workers × 2,000 × 8.2 KB = ~394 MB.

At 16:38 UTC, 4 Celery workers are OOMKilled (2,000 concurrent task initializations per pod — GPU context setup, tokenizer load — exhausted the 32 GB container memory limit). RabbitMQ requeues their 4 × 2,000 = 8,000 unacked messages to the queue head. The remaining 20 workers' prefetch buffers are already saturated — they cannot absorb the requeued messages. RabbitMQ's internal quorum queue unacked-tracking expands beyond the vm_memory_high_watermark_absolute threshold:

  2026-05-27T16:43:18.224Z [warning] <0.232.0> vm_memory_monitor: Memory alarm set on node rabbit@rabbitmq-0.rabbitmq-nodes.rabbitmq.svc. Memory used: 27,189,412,864 bytes (41.8% of 64 GB). Threshold: 26,388,279,296 (40.0%)
  2026-05-27T16:43:18.226Z [warning] <0.232.0> rabbit_alarm: Alert: memory on rabbit@rabbitmq-0. All publishers will be BLOCKED.

All 14 Pika connections from the coordinator service immediately receive a `Connection.Blocked` AMQP frame. The coordinator's publish path:

  # coordinator.py:142
  channel.basic_publish(exchange=exchange, routing_key=routing_key, body=body,
    properties=pika.BasicProperties(delivery_mode=2), mandatory=True)
  channel.wait_for_confirms_or_die(timeout=5.0)  # BUG: raises AMQPError after 5s on blocked connection

  except pika.exceptions.AMQPError as e:
      raise ServiceUnavailableError('Queue unavailable')

`wait_for_confirms_or_die(timeout=5.0)` times out on a blocked connection since RabbitMQ will not deliver confirms while the alarm is active. Every job submission raises `ServiceUnavailableError`. The Kubernetes liveness probe (GET /healthz, 3 consecutive failures → restart) begins OOMKilling coordinator pods that return HTTP 503 — the pods are memory-healthy but the health endpoint gates on queue publish reachability. Three of 20 coordinator pods enter CrashLoopBackOff, each restart triggering a new Pika connection storm.

GKE Cloud Monitoring (16:43–17:01 UTC):
  rabbitmq.queue.messages.ready on ml-inference-tasks: 847,294 → 849,221 (stalled, not draining)
  rabbitmq.cluster.memory_used_bytes: 27.2 GB (above 25.6 GB alarm threshold)
  rabbitmq.connection.blocked: 14
  ml-inference-coordinator error_rate: 100% for 17 minutes
  3 coordinator pods in CrashLoopBackOff
  Celery workers: 20 remaining pods at 100% CPU processing prefetched backlog

340K inference jobs missed during the 17-minute stall. The downstream recommendation engine falls back to a 4-hour-old stale prediction cache. Estimated revenue impact: $220K from degraded ML personalization CTR and a 3.4% click-through drop on the primary recommendation feed.
items.exercise:
1. Triage — differentiate memory alarm from cluster failure: `kubectl exec -it rabbitmq-0 -n rabbitmq -- rabbitmqctl status | grep -A5 alarms` → `{resource_alarm,true,memory,rabbit@rabbitmq-0}`. Confirm cluster topology intact: `rabbitmqctl cluster_status` — all 3 nodes running, no network partition. List blocked connections: `rabbitmqctl list_connections name blocked_by | grep memory` → 14 coordinator connections. This is producer backpressure (recoverable), not a broker crash. Rule out coordinator OOM: `kubectl top pod -n ml-inference | grep coordinator` — memory at baseline. The HTTP 503s originate from the application-layer ServiceUnavailableError on publish timeout, not container memory pressure. Do NOT restart RabbitMQ — the alarm clears when memory drops naturally.
2. Immediate mitigation — drain the unacked message set by scaling Celery to 0: `kubectl scale deployment celery-worker -n ml-inference --replicas=0`. This forcibly disconnects all consumer channels; RabbitMQ requeues their 20 × 2,000 = 40,000 unacked messages back to disk storage (quorum queue durability), removing them from the in-memory unacked tracking set. After 15s, trigger an immediate memory reassessment: `kubectl exec -it rabbitmq-0 -n rabbitmq -- rabbitmqctl eval 'rabbit_vm_memory_monitor:update().'`. Watch alarm clear: `rabbitmqctl status | grep alarms` — expect empty within 30s. Coordinator error rate drops to 0% within 10s of the alarm clearing as `Connection.Blocked` frames are followed by `Connection.Unblocked`.
3. Scale Celery back with corrected prefetch — for tasks with duration > 1s, Celery documentation explicitly recommends `CELERYD_PREFETCH_MULTIPLIER=1`: `kubectl set env deployment/celery-worker -n ml-inference CELERYD_PREFETCH_MULTIPLIER=1 CELERYD_CONCURRENCY=8 && kubectl scale deployment celery-worker -n ml-inference --replicas=12`. New unacked ceiling: 12 pods × 1 multiplier × 8 concurrency = 96 max simultaneously unacked. This is 500× less than the peak of 48,000 and stays far within RabbitMQ's normal memory budget.
4. Fix Pika Connection.Blocked handling — the coordinator should pause publishing during a transient memory alarm rather than raising ServiceUnavailableError: Register block/unblock callbacks on the Pika connection in coordinator.py: `blocked_event = threading.Event(); connection.add_on_connection_blocked_callback(lambda conn, method: blocked_event.set()); connection.add_on_connection_unblocked_callback(lambda conn, method: blocked_event.clear())`. Before each `basic_publish` call, add: `if blocked_event.is_set(): logger.warning('RabbitMQ publisher blocked — waiting up to 90s for memory alarm to clear'); blocked_event.wait(timeout=90.0)`. Raise only if the alarm persists > 90s (broker-level failure, not transient backpressure). Replace `wait_for_confirms_or_die(timeout=5.0)` with a retry loop that tolerates `AMQPConnectionForced` and waits for unblock before retrying delivery.
5. Decouple liveness probe from queue publish path: The liveness probe must not gate on external dependencies. Split health endpoints: `/healthz/live` returns HTTP 200 if the process is responsive (no external checks); `/healthz/ready` returns HTTP 200 only when the Pika connection is open and `blocked_event.is_set()=False`. Update the Kubernetes probe config: use `/healthz/live` for `livenessProbe` and `/healthz/ready` for `readinessProbe`. This removes coordinator pods from the load balancer during a publish block (correct behavior) without triggering a restart storm that compounds the incident.
6. Validate: After scale-up with `CELERYD_PREFETCH_MULTIPLIER=1`, confirm `rabbitmqctl list_queues name messages_unacknowledged` shows ml-inference-tasks at ≤96. Run `rabbitmqctl status | grep alarms` — empty (alarm cleared). `kubectl get pods -n ml-inference | grep coordinator` — 0 pods in CrashLoopBackOff. Cloud Monitoring: `rabbitmq.connection.blocked = 0`. ml-inference-tasks queue depth begins draining at ~96 tasks/12s = ~480 tasks/min. Confirm model inference results appear in the downstream Redis feature cache within 3 minutes.
7. Prevent: (a) Add RabbitMQ memory alarm PagerDuty alert: `rabbitmq.cluster.memory_alarm=true` for 30s → P1 'RabbitMQ publisher block active'. This fires within 30s vs the 6-minute human discovery delay in this incident. (b) Add prefetch guard in Celery worker entrypoint script: `if int(os.environ.get('CELERYD_PREFETCH_MULTIPLIER', 1)) > 50: sys.exit('CELERYD_PREFETCH_MULTIPLIER > 50 is disallowed for ML tasks with duration > 1s — max unacked would be {}; review Celery docs on prefetch for long-running tasks'.format(replicas * 50 * concurrency))`. Block the pod from starting with unsafe config. (c) Enable lazy queues on ml-inference-tasks: `rabbitmqctl set_policy lazy-ml 'ml-inference-tasks' '{'queue-mode':'lazy'}' --apply-to queues`. Lazy queues persist messages to disk on arrival and only load into RAM on delivery, decoupling queue depth from RabbitMQ RAM usage. For 849K messages × 8.2 KB = ~7 GB total, lazy mode keeps most of that off-heap and prevents the memory alarm from ever triggering during normal accumulation. (d) Add GKE alert: `rabbitmq.cluster.memory_used_bytes / 68719476736 > 0.80` for 2 min → P2 'RabbitMQ approaching memory alarm'. Fires with 12% RAM headroom before the blocking threshold (vs 0% headroom when the alarm fires).

items.drill: Temporal Workflow Sticky Queue Drain Stall — ML Training Pipeline Timeout Cascade During Worker Rolling Restart
items.layer: Distributed Systems (Temporal 1.22.4 / Go SDK 1.24.2 / GKE 1.29 / Cassandra 4.1)
items.scenario:
At 11:15 UTC, a Kubernetes 1.28→1.29 rolling upgrade begins on the ml-training-worker Deployment (8 pods, GKE us-central1, n2-standard-32 with attached NVIDIA A100-80GB, 92 active TrainModelWorkflow instances distributed across pods). Your Temporal 1.22.4 cluster (3-node StatefulSet, Cassandra 4.1 backend, namespace temporal-system) orchestrates long-running ML training jobs — each workflow runs 4–8 hours with 15–20 activity steps spanning data download, preprocessing, distributed training, evaluation, and S3 artifact upload.

Worker registration in worker_factory.go:

  // worker_factory.go:94
  opts := worker.Options{
    MaxConcurrentWorkflowTaskPollers: 8,
    MaxConcurrentActivityTaskPollers: 16,
    StickyScheduleToStartTimeout:     0, // intended to disable; MAPS TO INFINITE in Go SDK 1.24.x
  }
  w := worker.New(temporalClient, "ml-training-task-queue", opts)

In Temporal Go SDK 1.24.x, StickyScheduleToStartTimeout=0 sets an infinite sticky wait — not 'disabled'. The Temporal server's default (5s) only applies when the field is entirely omitted. This mis-configuration has been dormant for 8 months; previous rolling restarts were done manually with 30-second drain windows between pods.

The rolling upgrade replaces pods in 90-second intervals. As each pod is terminated (11:15–11:22 UTC), Temporal assigns its 23 active workflows to that pod's sticky task queue — a private queue keyed on the pod's identity. With no timeout, the server never redirects these workflows to the normal shared task queue. The 4 replacement pods create fresh sticky queues and service only NEW incoming workflows. At 11:20 UTC, the 4 × 23 = 92 stuck workflows breach their 5-minute activity ScheduleToStartTimeout:

  2026-05-28T11:20:44.231Z WARN  temporal-server WorkflowTaskTimedOut
    WorkflowType=TrainModelWorkflow WorkflowID=train-model-run-4891
    TaskQueue=__sticky_TrainModelWorkflow_ml-training-worker-64d7b8-9x2pz
    TimeoutType=ScheduleToStart (waited 4m43s; no pollers)
  2026-05-28T11:20:44.512Z ERROR app: activity timeout workflowID=train-model-run-4891
    activityType=DistributedTrainActivity error="ActivityTaskTimedOut: timeoutType:SCHEDULE_TO_START"

PagerDuty fires at 11:20:47 UTC: 'ml-training-orchestrator-prod workflow_timeout_rate 18.4% (threshold: 1%)'.

92 workflows enter TIMED_OUT state. A critical secondary failure: their GPU-backed training activities continue running. Temporal activity heartbeats (sent every 60s) are stateless RPC calls — they succeed even after the parent workflow is dead. The A100 nodes remain allocated and billing. GKE Cloud Monitoring (11:15–11:55 UTC):
  temporal_workflow_execution_completed{status='timed_out', namespace='ml-training'}: 92
  GKE node pool ml-a100-training active node count: 92 → 184 (orphaned activities hold 92 extra nodes)
  temporal_workflow_task_schedule_to_start_latency p99: 5.1s → +Inf
  Training job completion rate: 98.7% → 81.3%
  Orphaned GPU compute cost: 92 A100 nodes × avg 5.8 hr remaining × $32.77/hr = $17,499

Business impact: 92 ML training runs require full restart (8+ hours each), 7 recommendation models miss their scheduled refresh window, $17,499 in orphaned GPU compute charges, and 3 hours of investigation time connecting the K8s rolling upgrade to Temporal sticky queue behavior — no alert for orphaned activities existed.
items.exercise:
1. Diagnose: List all timed-out workflows from the incident window: `tctl workflow list --namespace ml-training --status TimedOut --start-time '2026-05-28T11:15:00Z'`. Inspect one: `tctl workflow show -w train-model-run-4891 --namespace ml-training | tail -20` — last event is WorkflowTaskTimedOut with TimeoutType=ScheduleToStart and TaskQueue pointing to __sticky_*_ml-training-worker-64d7b8-9x2pz (a terminated pod). Run `tctl taskqueue describe --task-queue __sticky_TrainModelWorkflow_ml-training-worker-64d7b8-9x2pz --namespace ml-training` → pollers: 0. Identify orphaned activities burning GPU: `kubectl get pods -n ml-training | grep train-activity | wc -l` → 184 (should be 92). Confirm SDK root cause: inspect Go SDK source for worker.Options.StickyScheduleToStartTimeout — in v1.24.x, value 0 maps to pb.DurationToProto(math.MaxInt64) in converters.go (infinite), not 'disabled'. Check worker_factory.go:94.
2. Stop orphaned GPU spend immediately: Enumerate timed-out workflow IDs: `tctl workflow list --namespace ml-training --status TimedOut -o json | jq -r '.[].execution.workflowId' > /tmp/timed_out_ids.txt`. For each workflow find and terminate its running child activities: `tctl workflow terminate --namespace ml-training --workflow-id <activity-run-id> --reason 'orphaned-activity-sticky-queue-incident-2026-05-28'`. Monitor node pool: `kubectl get nodes -l cloud.google.com/gke-nodepool=ml-a100-training --watch` — count drops from 184 → 92 within 90s as Cluster Autoscaler reclaims idle A100 nodes, stopping the $32.77/hr bleed per node.
3. Hotfix StickyScheduleToStartTimeout in worker_factory.go:94: Change `StickyScheduleToStartTimeout: 0` to `StickyScheduleToStartTimeout: 10 * time.Second`. Omitting the field is equivalent and inherits the Temporal server default (5s). Deploy via rolling restart — now safe because the 10s timeout will redirect stuck workflows to the normal task queue within one interval of each pod termination.
4. Re-run the 92 failed training workflows: `tctl workflow reset --namespace ml-training --query 'WorkflowType="TrainModelWorkflow" AND ExecutionStatus="TimedOut" AND StartTime > "2026-05-28T11:15:00Z"' --reset-type FirstWorkflowTask --reason 'sticky-queue-drain-recovery-2026-05-28'`. FirstWorkflowTask replays from the beginning — appropriate for training jobs that should not resume mid-run. Monitor: `tctl workflow list --namespace ml-training --status Running | wc -l` should climb back toward ~500 as reset workflows re-queue.
5. Validate: In staging, trigger a rolling restart after the hotfix: `kubectl rollout restart deployment/ml-training-worker -n ml-training-staging`. Watch: `tctl workflow list --namespace ml-training-staging --status TimedOut` — expect 0 during the entire restart. Verify sticky drain: after each pod termination, `tctl taskqueue describe --task-queue __sticky_TrainModelWorkflow_<terminated-pod> --namespace ml-training-staging` shows 0 workflows within 10s as they redirect to the normal queue. GPU node count stays flat throughout.
6. Prevent: (a) Add Temporal metric alert: `temporal_workflow_execution_completed{status='timed_out', namespace='ml-training'}` rate > 2/min for 1 minute → PagerDuty P1 'Temporal workflow timeout spike'. Fires within 90s of the first timeout. (b) Add a pre-rolling-restart gate in the deployment pipeline: before terminating each pod, query its sticky queue depth via Temporal API and wait up to 30s for drain or redirect; if depth > 0 after 30s, halt rollout and page on-call. (c) Add a Go lint rule flagging `StickyScheduleToStartTimeout: 0` in worker.Options — the zero value is counterintuitive (0 = infinite, not 'disabled') and has caused multiple production incidents across Temporal adopters. (d) Add orphaned activity detector: Cloud Scheduler → Cloud Function every 5 minutes queries Temporal for activities whose parent workflow status is TimedOut or Terminated, auto-terminates them, and pages PagerDuty P2 'orphaned Temporal activities detected'. (e) For 4-8 hour ML training workflows, disable sticky execution entirely: `DisableStickyExecution: true` in worker.Options. The ~20ms scheduling latency benefit from sticky execution is irrelevant at this timescale, and the failure mode during rolling restarts is completely eliminated.

items.drill: Azure Event Hubs Partition Ownership Thundering Herd — HPA Oscillation Triggers Blob Storage Throttle, Halts ML Feature Ingestion 22 Minutes
items.layer: Distributed Systems (Azure Event Hubs / Java Spring Boot / Azure AKS / Azure Blob Storage)
items.scenario:
At 15:22 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'feature-ingestion-service-prod consumer_lag_events 4,200,000 (threshold: 100,000)'. Your Java 21 Spring Boot feature ingestion service (azure-messaging-eventhubs-checkpointstore-blob 5.18.0, 8 pods on Azure AKS 1.29, Standard_D8s_v3, eastus) processes real-time ML inference signals from Azure Event Hubs namespace ml-events-prod (32 partitions, 7-day retention, ~85,000 events/min). Partition ownership state is stored in Azure Blob Storage (ml-checkpoints-prod/feature-ingestion/ownership/) using 30-second TTL lease blobs with ETag-based optimistic concurrency.

At 14:58 UTC, a burst of EU-timezone users triggered 8 seconds of 93% pod CPU utilization. A HorizontalPodAutoscaler (targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 80, stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0 — misconfigured during a recent 'simplification' refactor) fired an immediate scale-out from 8 → 48 pods. CPU normalized to 18% within 30s, but HPA with stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0 scaled back to 8 pods 2 minutes later. As 40 pods terminated simultaneously (SIGTERM, terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30), all 40 released their partition ownership blobs atomically. The 8 surviving pods detected 32 partition ownership gaps and simultaneously issued Azure Blob Storage PutBlob calls to claim them:

  2026-06-04T15:00:43.112Z WARN c.a.m.e.EventProcessorClient - Partition 07 claim failed [attempt=1]: 412 Precondition Failed (If-Match: etag-a71c44d; server etag: etag-b82d19f)
  2026-06-04T15:00:43.114Z WARN c.a.m.e.EventProcessorClient - Partition 12 claim failed [attempt=1]: 412 Precondition Failed (If-Match: etag-c93e21a; server etag: etag-d04f38b)
  2026-06-04T15:00:43.829Z WARN c.a.m.e.EventProcessorClient - Partition 07 claim failed [attempt=2]: 412 Precondition Failed (retrying in 2048ms, jitter=+841ms)
  [...847 identical warnings across all 8 pods over 6 minutes...]

Each failed claim triggers a full BlobCheckpointStore.listOwnership() scan across all 32 partition blobs — a read-amplification blast of 32 requests per pod per retry. With 8 pods retrying on 200–3000ms jittered backoff, blob request rate spikes to 31,200/min (78x baseline). Azure Blob Storage begins returning 503 throttle errors at 15:01:07 UTC. The failed claims also raise pod CPU (ownership rebalancing is CPU-intensive), which retriggered the HPA to scale back out to 48 pods at 15:02:44 UTC — beginning the scale oscillation cycle that repeats 7 times.

Azure Monitor (14:58–15:22 UTC):
  eventhub/outgoing_messages: 85,000/min → 0 (zero consumer progress)
  eventhub/consumer_lag_offset: 0 → 1,870,000 events (growing 85K/min)
  storage/blob_transactions: 400/min → 31,200/min
  storage/throttling_error_responses: 0 → 2,847/min (Azure Blob account-level limit: 20,000 req/s)
  storage/success_e2e_latency_p99: 12ms → 8,400ms (throttle-induced queuing)
  aks/pod_count{deploy=feature-ingestion}: oscillating 8 → 48 → 8 → 48 × 7 cycles

Partition 07 ownership blob shows 22 owner changes in 8 minutes (healthy: 0–2/hour). Zero partitions were actively consumed during this window — every pod was in rebalancing state.

Root cause: Two compounding failures. (1) HPA stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0 removes the scale-in cooldown, allowing recursive oscillation when the rebalancing CPU load itself re-triggers HPA. (2) azure-messaging-eventhubs 5.18.0 BlobCheckpointStore.claimOwnership() is not linearizable under simultaneous mass-release: 8 pods reading stale ownership state in parallel all select the same unclaimed partitions, all issue concurrent PutBlob with mismatched ETags, all fail, all retry — a classic thundering herd against a shared lock store. Azure Blob Storage does not have a distributed mutex primitive; the SDK's jittered backoff eventually converges, but convergence time at 8 concurrent retriers over 32 slots is O(minutes), not O(seconds). Business impact: 22 minutes of zero feature ingestion; ML serving falls back to 45-minute-old Redis-cached features; CTR −6.2% for 3 personalization models; 14 ML A/B experiments cannot record inference signals, invalidating their measurement windows; $220K estimated revenue impact.
items.exercise:
1. Triage — confirm ownership storm vs Event Hubs broker failure: Azure Monitor → Event Hubs namespace ml-events-prod → Incoming Requests: producer write rate is normal (~85K/min), confirming the broker is healthy. Outgoing Messages = 0 means consumers are not receiving. Check AKS pod count oscillation: kubectl get events -n ml-serving --field-selector reason=SuccessfulRescale | tail -20 — confirms rapid HPA scale-up/scale-down cycles. Confirm blob throttling: Azure Monitor → Storage Account ml-checkpoints-prod → Transactions by Response Type — filter 'ThrottlingError' shows 2,847/min starting at 15:01:07. Confirm ownership churn: az storage blob list --container-name feature-ingestion --prefix ownership/ --account-name ml-checkpoints-prod | jq '.[0:5][] | {name, etag, last_modified}' — recent last_modified timestamps within seconds on every partition blob confirms the thundering herd. This is an HPA oscillation + checkpoint blob storm, not a broker outage.
2. Break the HPA oscillation cycle: Immediately disable HPA automatic scaling to freeze the pod count: kubectl annotate hpa feature-ingestion-hpa autoscaling.kubernetes.io/managed-by- -n ml-serving && kubectl scale hpa feature-ingestion-hpa --min-replicas=8 --max-replicas=8 -n ml-serving. Alternatively, patch spec.minReplicas=spec.maxReplicas=8 via kubectl patch. This stops new pods from being scheduled or terminated, giving the 8 existing pods a stable environment to converge on partition ownership. Critical: do NOT manually delete or restart pods while ownership is contested — this restarts the entire thundering herd.
3. Wait for ownership convergence: After the HPA freeze, the BlobCheckpointStore jittered backoff will converge within 2–5 minutes as contention decreases. Monitor convergence: watch kubectl logs -l app=feature-ingestion -n ml-serving --since=2m | grep -c 'claim failed' — count should decrease from ~847/min to 0 per 2-min window. Verify all 32 partitions are owned by watching: az storage blob list --container-name feature-ingestion --prefix ownership/ --account-name ml-checkpoints-prod | jq 'map(select(.metadata.ownerid != null)) | length' — expect 32. EventHubs/OutgoingMessages should resume within 60–90s of full ownership assignment.
4. Drain the consumer lag: Once all 32 partitions are claimed and receiving events, the 4.2M event backlog will drain at ~85K events/min (normal processing rate). At 85K/min capacity with no additional backlog from continued ingestion, full drain takes ~50 minutes. To accelerate: temporarily increase the pod count to 24 (3 pods/partition) via the HPA minReplicas after ownership has stabilized — parallelism within a partition is partition-count bounded, but 24 pods vs 8 pods can triple throughput if upstream Event Hubs throughput units allow. Monitor EventHubs/ConsumerLag every 5 minutes — it should decrease steadily.
5. Fix HPA stabilizationWindowSeconds: Add a scale-in stabilization window to the HPA spec: kubectl patch hpa feature-ingestion-hpa -n ml-serving -p '{"spec":{"behavior":{"scaleDown":{"stabilizationWindowSeconds":300,"policies":[{"type":"Percent","value":25,"periodSeconds":60}]},"scaleUp":{"stabilizationWindowSeconds":60,"policies":[{"type":"Pods","value":4,"periodSeconds":60}]}}}}'. The scale-down window of 300s (5 min) prevents immediate reaction to transient CPU spikes from rebalancing events. The scale-up policy limits growth to 4 pods/minute — prevents a 40-pod simultaneous launch. The scale-down policy caps at 25%/min — prevents a 40-pod simultaneous teardown. Commit this to the Helm chart values.yaml under hpa.behavior.
6. Prevent: (a) Replace BlobCheckpointStore with a leader-election-based ownership strategy for large partition counts: use Azure Table Storage with PartitionKey=consumerGroup/partitionId and RowKey=leaseId, with a single conditional replace per partition — Table Storage's strongly-consistent entity-level transactions eliminate the ETag race without throttling at blob account level. Azure EventHubs 5.19.0+ supports a TableCheckpointStore implementation as a drop-in replacement. (b) Add Azure Monitor alert on storage/throttling_error_responses > 50/min for 2 consecutive 1-minute periods → PagerDuty P2 'checkpoint blob store throttled'. This fires 20 minutes before the consumer lag becomes critical. (c) Add an HPA guard metric: emit a custom metric feature_ingestion_ownership_rebalance_rate from the application (DogStatsd or Azure Monitor custom metric) and add a HPA metric selector: scale down is suspended when ownership_rebalance_rate > 0. The pods freeze at stable count until rebalancing completes, then scale-down proceeds. (d) Integration test for scale-in: in a staging AKS namespace, scale feature-ingestion from 32 pods to 4 pods and assert EventHubs/OutgoingMessages > 0 (non-zero consumer progress) within 90s of scale-in completing — catches any future ownership convergence regression before production.

items.drill: GCP Pub/Sub Dead Letter Topic Infinite Retry Storm — Protobuf Breaking Schema Change Stalls ML Event Pipeline for 31 Minutes
items.layer: Distributed Systems (GCP Cloud Pub/Sub / Go 1.22 / Protocol Buffers / GKE)
items.scenario:
At 16:44 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'ml-event-pipeline-prod dead_letter_message_count 47,200 (threshold: 500)'. Your Go 1.22 ML feature ingestion service on GKE (ml-event-consumer-prod, 3 replicas, 8 worker goroutines per pod, ~1,800 msg/sec on the ml-events-primary Pub/Sub subscription, us-central1) has been NAKing 3% of messages since 16:38 UTC. The subscription has max_delivery_attempts=5; exhausted messages route to the dead-letter topic ml-events-dead-letter.

At 18:02 UTC the previous day, the Python publisher service deployed UserInteractionEvent Protobuf v1.4.0, which removed the deprecated `deprecated_session_context` field (proto field number 12). The Go consumer's generated code was compiled against v1.3.0 which never declared `reserved 12;`. The proto library silently handles the absent field — proto.Unmarshal returns no error. But the Go handler calls `features.ExtractSession(event.GetDeprecatedSessionContext())`, which returns `features.ErrMissingSessionContext` when the context struct is zero-valued. The handler propagates the error and calls `msg.Nack()`.

DLQ configuration: ml-events-dead-letter subscription, ack_deadline_seconds=10, no retry policy set (GCP default: minimum_backoff=0s, maximum_backoff=0s — immediate redeliver on NAK). The DLQ subscriber is the SAME container image (registry.gcr.io/ml-platform/event-consumer:v2.1.4) running the same broken handler. Every message the DLQ consumer processes triggers the same ErrMissingSessionContext → msg.Nack() → GCP redelivers within 10s — a closed infinite retry loop.

By 16:51 UTC, the DLQ subscription outstanding message count reaches the GCP per-subscription quota (100,000 outstanding messages). GCP applies flow-control backpressure to the primary subscription. Primary throughput drops from 1,800 → 0 msg/sec:

  gcp_pubsub_subscription_num_outstanding_messages{subscription="ml-events-dead-letter"}: 100000 (at hard cap)
  gcp_pubsub_subscription_num_undelivered_messages{subscription="ml-events-primary"}: 2,847,200 (growing +1800/s)
  gcp_pubsub_subscription_oldest_unacked_message_age{subscription="ml-events-primary"}: 420s
  gcp_pubsub_subscription_pull_message_operation_count{subscription="ml-events-dead-letter"}: 8,320/min

Go consumer logs (repeating ~8K/min on DLQ subscriber):
  2026-06-12T16:51:04.221Z ERROR event-consumer features/session.go:94
    ErrMissingSessionContext: deprecated_session_context absent or empty
    {"message_id": "7312984234-JXMQ", "delivery_attempt": 5, "subscription": "ml-events-dead-letter"}
  2026-06-12T16:51:04.222Z WARN event-consumer pubsub/handler.go:41
    message NACK {"message_id": "7312984234-JXMQ", "reason": "ErrMissingSessionContext"}

At 17:15 UTC (31 min), 3.24M events are blocked on the primary pipeline. Feature freshness SLA (< 5 min old) is violated for 14 ML ranking models; models fall back to 47-minute-old Redis-cached features. CTR drops 4.2%; signal gaps corrupt two active A/B experiment measurement windows.
items.exercise:
1. Triage — identify the DLQ tight loop, not the primary pipeline: Check GCP Cloud Monitoring → ml-events-dead-letter → subscription/pull_message_operation_count. If this metric is at 8,000+/min while ml-events-primary pull count is 0, the DLQ is in a tight retry loop starving the primary. Confirm: gcloud pubsub subscriptions describe ml-events-dead-letter --format=json | jq '.retryPolicy' — if null, GCP is using 0s minimum_backoff. Check primary backpressure: gcloud pubsub subscriptions describe ml-events-primary | grep numUndeliveredMessages — growing unboundedly confirms DLQ saturation is the cause. Rule out publisher failure: subscription/num_undelivered_messages growing on the primary confirms messages ARE being published (broker is healthy, consumers are blocked).
2. Immediate mitigation — pause the DLQ subscriber: kubectl scale deployment ml-event-consumer-dlq -n ml-serving --replicas=0. This stops all DLQ pull attempts within 10s (pending pulls time out at ack_deadline_seconds). The GCP outstanding message count on the DLQ drops as in-flight deliveries expire; flow-control backpressure on the primary releases. Within 60s, primary consumers resume. Monitor: gcloud pubsub subscriptions describe ml-events-primary → numUndeliveredMessages should stop growing and begin decreasing. Do NOT delete DLQ messages — they are valid events that will be reprocessed after the fix.
3. Root fix — patch the proto and handler: (a) Add `reserved 12; reserved "deprecated_session_context";` to UserInteractionEvent.proto v1.4.1 — prevents future field reuse on number 12. Regenerate Go stubs. (b) Update features/session.go:94: replace the hard error on zero-value struct with a nil/isEmpty guard: `if ctx := event.GetDeprecatedSessionContext(); ctx != nil && ctx.GetSessionId() != "" { if err := features.ExtractSession(ctx); err != nil { return err } }` — graceful no-op on absent deprecated field. (c) Deploy the fixed image (v2.1.5) to both the primary and DLQ consumer deployments via rolling restart. Validate in staging: publish a synthetic v1.4.0-format message (no field 12) against the new binary and confirm it is ACKed without error.
4. Drain the DLQ after fix deployment: Restore DLQ consumers with the fixed image: kubectl set image deployment/ml-event-consumer-dlq ml-event-consumer-dlq=registry.gcr.io/ml-platform/event-consumer:v2.1.5 -n ml-serving && kubectl scale deployment ml-event-consumer-dlq -n ml-serving --replicas=3. Monitor: gcloud pubsub subscriptions describe ml-events-dead-letter | grep numUndeliveredMessages — should drain from 47,200 to 0 within ~5 min at 1,800 msg/sec throughput with 3 replicas. Confirm zero NAKs: if subscription/num_outstanding_messages stays well below cap and pull_message_operation_count normalizes to baseline, the fix is verified. Feature freshness restores once oldest_unacked_message_age on the primary drops below 5 min.
5. Add DLQ retry backoff to prevent future tight-loop redelivery: gcloud pubsub subscriptions modify-push-config ml-events-dead-letter --min-retry-delay=60 --max-retry-delay=600. This caps redelivery frequency to once every 60–600s (exponential backoff). Even with a broken DLQ consumer, the maximum NAK rate becomes 1 per 60s per message — 8,300 NAK/sec becomes ~1 NAK/min per message, making flow-control saturation impossible. Apply this to ALL dead-letter subscriptions as a platform policy: gcloud pubsub subscriptions list --filter='name~dead-letter' --format='value(name)' | xargs -I{} gcloud pubsub subscriptions modify-push-config {} --min-retry-delay=30 --max-retry-delay=300.
6. Prevent: (a) DLQ subscriber image pinning: the DLQ consumer Deployment uses a separate stable tag pinned at least 1 release behind the primary — so a newly broken primary image never simultaneously affects the DLQ escape hatch. (b) CI Protobuf breaking-change detection: add `buf breaking --against '.git#branch=main'` to the CI pipeline. Any field removal without `reserved` declaration fails the build before merge — catches this class of breaking change at PR time. (c) Add Cloud Monitoring alert: subscription/oldest_unacked_message_age{subscription="ml-events-primary"} > 300s → PagerDuty P1 (fires before DLQ saturation causes backpressure). Also: subscription/pull_message_operation_count{subscription=~".*dead-letter.*"} > 5000/min → PagerDuty P2 'DLQ tight loop detected'. (d) Monthly DLQ audit: gcloud pubsub subscriptions list --filter='name~dead-letter' --format=json | jq '.[] | {name, retryPolicy}' — any subscription with null retryPolicy gets minimum_backoff ≥ 30s added as a blocking item before the next sprint. (e) Schema compatibility contract: any proto field removal requires (i) reserved declaration, (ii) a consumer deploy that handles both old and new format before the publisher deploy — enforced via a deploy sequence gate in the release runbook.

items.drill: DynamoDB Hot Partition — Sequential User ID Batch Scan Exhausts Adaptive Capacity, Stales ML Feature Reads for 2.3M Users
items.layer: Distributed Systems (DynamoDB / AWS Lambda / Python / ML Feature Pipeline)
items.scenario:
At 14:09 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'rec-engine-prod feature_fetch_error_rate 23.4% (threshold: 2%)'. Your Python recommendation engine (AWS Lambda, 200 reserved concurrency, us-east-1) reads user features from DynamoDB table `user-features-prod` (provisioned: 6,000 RCU / 2,000 WCU, 120M items, 3.4TB). Features include embedding vectors (1,536-float32, ~6KB/item), behavioral signals, and demographic segments consumed by 3 XGBoost ranking models.

At 13:48 UTC, the daily batch inference pipeline deployed — it reads user features to score next-day engagement predictions. The pipeline (24 Lambda workers, Python 3.12, boto3 1.34.79) iterates user IDs in sorted order: `[usr_00000001, usr_00000002, ..., usr_02300000]`.

DynamoDB MD5-based partition hashing normally distributes keys uniformly, but this sequential zero-padded 11-character prefix creates a hash collision cluster: ~63% of `usr_000XXXXX` IDs hash to the same 3 internal partition nodes. CloudWatch at 14:09 UTC:

  DynamoDB:ConsumedReadCapacityUnits     = 71,204 RCU/min (provisioned: 6,000 RCU/min)
  DynamoDB:ReadThrottledEvents           = 18,436 events/min (baseline: 0)
  DynamoDB:SuccessfulRequestLatency_p99  = 8,340ms (baseline: 12ms)
  Lambda:Errors{function=rec-engine}     = 23.4% (baseline: 0.1%)

The batch pipeline consumed 71,204 RCU/min — far exceeding provisioned 6,000 RCU/min. DynamoDB Adaptive Capacity absorbed the burst for the first 15 minutes (3× per-partition burst), but its credit pool exhausted at 14:03 UTC, causing a hard throttle on the 3 hot partitions. Real-time recommendation Lambda GetItem calls on those partitions now also throttle, returning ProvisionedThroughputExceededException. After 3 boto3 retries the Lambda falls back to ElastiCache Redis (TTL: 6h, last populated at 08:09 UTC) — serving 6-hour-stale features to all 2.3M DAUs.

One ranking model uses `user_activity_velocity_24h` — a rapidly changing feature. The stale 08:09 value predicts low activity for evening-peak users, causing the ranker to serve chronologically-sorted instead of personalized feeds. Personalized feed CTR drops 28% (4.2% → 3.0%). Engineering estimates $184K/hr in lost ad revenue. Business escalates at 14:21 UTC (12 min after initial alert).
items.exercise:
1. Diagnose — confirm hot partition throttling, not simply over-provisioned table: `ReadThrottledEvents > 0` while `ConsumedReadCapacityUnits` greatly exceeds provisioned capacity confirms per-partition limits are the bottleneck. Run: `aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics --namespace AWS/DynamoDB --metric-name ReadThrottledEvents --dimensions Name=TableName,Value=user-features-prod --start-time 2026-06-17T14:00:00Z --end-time 2026-06-17T14:15:00Z --period 60 --statistics Sum`. Enable DynamoDB Contributor Insights: `aws dynamodb update-contributor-insights --table-name user-features-prod --contributor-insights-action ENABLE` → CloudWatch Insights → Most Read Keys — should immediately show `usr_000XXXXX` dominating >60% of reads. Cross-reference: `aws logs filter-log-events --log-group-name /aws/lambda/batch-inference --filter-pattern '"GetItem" "usr_000"' | head -20` — confirms sequential IDs in batch reads. Baseline RCU before 13:48: ~800 RCU/min → confirms the batch pipeline is the 89× amplification source.
2. Immediate mitigation — pause the batch pipeline: `aws lambda put-function-concurrency --function-name batch-inference --reserved-concurrent-executions 0`. All 24 batch workers drain within 60 seconds. Adaptive Capacity burst credits begin replenishing (~300 RCU/partition/min). After 3–4 minutes, real-time recommendation Lambda reads succeed again. Monitor: `ReadThrottledEvents` drops to 0; `rec-engine` error rate drops from 23.4% → <1% within 90 seconds of batch pause. Force a real-time feature refresh for the highest-traffic users (top 100K by DAU) via a priority mini-batch using non-sequential user IDs that naturally distribute across partitions — restoring personalized feed quality for the most impactful user cohort within 8 minutes.
3. Fix the hot partition root cause — randomize batch read order: The core issue is that sequential scan pattern maps 63% of IDs to 3 partitions. Replace sequential ordering with a hash-distributed access pattern:
```python
# Before (hotspot pattern):
user_ids = sorted(all_user_ids)  # usr_00000001, usr_00000002, ...

# After (uniform partition distribution):
user_ids = list(all_user_ids)
random.shuffle(user_ids)  # Distributes reads across all DynamoDB partitions
```
Rate-limit the batch pipeline to stay under 50% of provisioned RCU. Use boto3 adaptive retry: `Config(retries={'mode': 'adaptive', 'max_attempts': 6})` — this implements automatic request rate throttling on ProvisionedThroughputExceededException rather than generating thundering-herd retries.
4. Durable fix — BatchGetItem with time-spreading via SQS: Replace the sequential GetItem loop with DynamoDB BatchGetItem (100 items/call) spread across a 20-minute window:
   - Chunk 2.3M users into 23,000 mini-batches of 100 users each
   - Enqueue mini-batches to SQS FIFO queue with 120ms inter-batch delays (27,800 RCU/min — under 6,000 provisioned + 3× burst ceiling with 1.5× headroom)
   - Total batch duration: ~46 minutes (acceptable for daily refresh)
   - SQS decoupling also allows graceful pause/resume if ReadThrottledEvents spike
5. Validate: After deploying shuffled read order and re-enabling batch pipeline (`aws lambda put-function-concurrency --function-name batch-inference --reserved-concurrent-executions 24`): `ReadThrottledEvents` = 0 over a 10-minute window. `ConsumedReadCapacityUnits` ≤ 300K/5min. Recommendation engine `feature_fetch_error_rate` < 0.1%. Contributor Insights 'Most Read Keys' — no single key prefix exceeds 5% of reads (previously 63%). Confirm CTR recovery: feature freshness monitor shows `user_activity_velocity_24h` lag < 2 min (vs 6h during incident).
6. Prevent: (a) Enable CloudWatch alarm: `ReadThrottledEvents > 100/min for 2 consecutive minutes → PagerDuty P2` — fires 11 minutes before adaptive capacity exhaustion. (b) Add pre-flight RCU budget check to the batch pipeline: estimate `rcu_per_min = num_users × avg_item_size_kb / 4 / batch_duration_min` and assert it is <40% of provisioned before starting. Block the job if the budget is exceeded without an explicit override. (c) Consider DynamoDB on-demand capacity mode for `user-features-prod` if batch patterns are inherently variable — on-demand auto-scales to 40,000 RCU/s instantly with no burst credit exhaustion (tradeoff: ~2.6× cost per RCU vs. provisioned at current baseline). (d) Add a dedicated `user-features-batch` table (on-demand, replicated via DynamoDB Streams + Lambda) for batch-exclusive reads — isolates batch traffic so real-time Lambda reads are never throttled by batch ingestion patterns regardless of batch access order.

items.drill: Kafka Streams Changelog Retention Expiry — State Store Wipeout After 73-Hour Maintenance Pause Zeroes ML Feature Vectors, Fraud Model Block Rate Collapses from 15.4% to 0.8%
items.layer: Distributed Systems (Kafka Streams 3.5.0 / Java 17 / AWS MSK / ECS Fargate)
items.scenario:
At 09:14 UTC Monday, PagerDuty fires: 'fraud-model-block-rate 0.8% (threshold: 12%)'. Your Java Kafka Streams 3.5.0 fraud pipeline on AWS ECS Fargate (8 tasks, fraud-pipeline-prod, 32 MSK partitions, us-east-1) processes 14,200 transactions/min and maintains 7 RocksDB state stores: user_txn_count_7d, merchant_risk_score_30d, user_spend_velocity_60min, etc. These features feed a LightGBM fraud model hosted on SageMaker (Triton inference) that historically blocks 15-18% of transactions daily.

Last Thursday, DevOps scaled the ECS service to 0 for a planned AWS region failover test. The test ran 73 hours before being cancelled. MSK changelog topics (e.g., fraud-pipeline-prod-user_txn_count_7d-changelog) were configured with log.retention.ms=86400000 (24h). After 73h, all changelog segments expired and were deleted.

When 8 tasks restart at 09:07 UTC Monday:

  Kafka Streams log (09:07:43 UTC):
    INFO  o.a.k.s.p.internals.StateDirectory - Starting from scratch for store user_txn_count_7d
    INFO  o.a.k.s.p.internals.StateDirectory - Starting from scratch for store merchant_risk_score_30d
    INFO  o.a.k.s.p.internals.StateDirectory - Starting from scratch for store user_spend_velocity_60min
    INFO  o.a.k.s.p.i.RecordCollector       - State restore for task 0_0 completed in 0ms (0 records)

All 32 state stores reset to zero. LightGBM receives feature vectors of all 0.0 — user_txn_count_7d=0 makes every user appear as a new account with zero transaction history and zero risk signal. Fraud scores collapse from the expected distribution (p50: 0.63, p99: 0.91) to near-uniform low values (p50: 0.042, p99: 0.087) — all below the 0.35 block threshold.

AWS CloudWatch at 09:14 UTC:
  fraud_model_block_rate:                0.8%   (7d baseline: 15.4%)
  kafka_streams_restore_records_total:   0      (expected: ~847M on full restore)
  fraud_model_prediction_p50_score:      0.042  (7d baseline: 0.63)
  fraud_transactions_blocked_per_minute: 11     (7d baseline: 2,184)

Zero HTTP errors — Triton is healthy, LightGBM returns valid scores — just from zeroed features. Over 4 hours before detection: 3.41M transactions processed, ~847K fraudulent transactions slip through the collapsed block rate, ~$829K in fraudulent charges approved.
items.exercise:
1. Triage — confirm state store wipeout, not model failure: SSH to ECS task and check RocksDB mount: ls -la /data/kafka-streams/fraud-pipeline-prod-user_txn_count_7d-rocksdb/ — empty directory confirms wipeout. Check JMX: kafka.streams:type=stream-state-metrics,store-id=user_txn_count_7d → size=0 on all tasks (expected: ~4.2M entries/partition). Confirm changelog expiry on MSK: kafka-topics.sh --bootstrap-server $MSK_BROKER --describe --topic fraud-pipeline-prod-user_txn_count_7d-changelog | grep LogSize — 0 bytes across all 32 partitions. Cross-check model vs feature failure: fraud_model_prediction_p50_score=0.042 (not NaN/5xx errors) distinguishes zeroed feature distribution from a model serving failure — Triton is healthy, features are the problem. Pull Kafka Streams app logs from 09:07 UTC: grep 'Starting from scratch' confirms every store reset to empty.
2. Immediate mitigation — halt model scoring and activate rule-based fallback: Set SageMaker A/B routing weight to 0% for fraud-model-prod; enable pre-configured velocity rules as fallback: >$500 single transaction → auto-review queue, >5 transactions within 5 minutes from the same user → auto-hold. Alert payment operations team to begin manual review of high-value transactions from the 4-hour window (09:07–09:14 UTC detection gap). This stops the fraud bleed within 2 minutes of the runbook step executing.
3. Restore state stores from S3 snapshot: If the pipeline writes daily RocksDB SSTables to s3://fraud-pipeline-backup/<date>/, configure a custom StateRestoreListener that loads Thursday midnight snapshot before the Kafka Streams app starts — restores ~73% of the 7-day user_txn_count_7d window in ~5 minutes. Replay the remaining 3 days of transactions-raw-prod from the Thursday offset (auto.offset.reset=earliest, isolated consumer group fraud-pipeline-restore). Full restore takes ~45 min at 14,200 events/min. Monitor progress: kafka.streams:type=stream-restore-consumer-metrics → records-consumed-rate should reach ~50,000/s.
4. Fix — extend changelog retention beyond maximum expected downtime: Apply to ALL changelog topics: kafka-topics.sh --list | grep 'changelog' | xargs -I{} kafka-configs.sh --bootstrap-server $MSK_BROKER --alter --entity-type topics --entity-name {} --add-config log.retention.ms=604800000 (7 days). Establish the rule: retention_ms must be > max_planned_downtime × 3 (safety factor). Set num.standby.replicas=1 in the Kafka Streams application configuration — standby replicas maintain warm shadow copies of state stores in separate tasks, enabling instantaneous restoration from the standby (0 records to replay) rather than full changelog replay.
5. Validate: JMX size for each store ≥ 4.2M entries per task. fraud_model_prediction_p50_score recovers to 0.55–0.65 range within 15 min of full restore. fraud_model_block_rate returns above 12% within 20 min. Validate data integrity: compare Thursday 23:00 UTC checkpoint row counts against restored state — should match within 0.5% across all 7 stores.
6. Prevent: (a) ECS task health gate blocks traffic until stores are warm — healthcheck command queries JMX: curl -s localhost:8080/actuator/health/kafka-streams | jq -e '.details.stores.user_txn_count_7d.size > 1000000' — ECS ALB routes no traffic until all stores pass the size threshold, preventing zeroed features from ever reaching the fraud model. (b) Fraud scoring pre-check — before each inference call, validate that the feature vector has fewer than 30% zero values; if not, route to rule-based fallback instead of the model. (c) MSK CloudWatch alarm: log_size_bytes{topic=~'.*changelog.*'} < 1MB AND consumer_group_lag{group='fraud-pipeline-prod'} = 0 → PagerDuty P1 'Changelog purged while consumer paused'. (d) Daily S3 RocksDB SSTable snapshot via custom RestoreListener (estimated $4.70/day for 8 partitions × 2.1GB average store size). (e) Pre-maintenance runbook gate: require explicit sign-off if planned downtime > log.retention.ms / 2; capture max consumer group lag must be 0 before any ECS scale-to-zero operation.

items.drill: Kafka KRaft Controller Voter Set Mismatch — PVC Deletion Prevents Replacement Controller from Joining Quorum, Blocks All Topic Mutations for 27 Minutes
items.layer: Distributed Systems (Apache Kafka 3.7 KRaft / GKE StatefulSets / Confluent Platform 7.6 / Java 21)
items.scenario:
At 09:17 UTC, PagerDuty fires: kafka_controller_quorum_leader_id == -1 for > 90s on your data-platform Kafka cluster (GKE us-central1, Confluent Platform 7.6.1, migrated from ZooKeeper to KRaft in Q3 2025, 9 brokers + 3 dedicated controllers as a StatefulSet). The alert fires during a routine replacement of kafka-kraft-controller-1 after its underlying GKE PersistentVolume was reported corrupted during a node maintenance window. The on-call SRE followed the standard pod-replacement procedure: delete the PVC, delete the pod, let Kubernetes recreate both — a procedure that was safe in ZooKeeper mode.

Logs on the new kafka-kraft-controller-1 pod (09:15:12 UTC):

  [2026-06-24 09:15:12,441] ERROR [RaftManager id=1] Error in raft IO thread (KafkaRaftClient)
  org.apache.kafka.common.errors.NotLeaderOrFollowerException: The metadata stored in meta.properties differs from the expected quorum voter set.
  Expected: {0=kafka-kraft-controller-0.kafka-kraft-controller-headless:9093, 1=kafka-kraft-controller-1.kafka-kraft-controller-headless:9093, 2=kafka-kraft-controller-2.kafka-kraft-controller-headless:9093}
  Actual: {} (EMPTY — unformatted storage)
  [2026-06-24 09:15:12,442] FATAL [RaftManager id=1] Exiting process due to fatal error
  java.lang.RuntimeException: Broker starting with invalid meta.properties: cluster.id not present

Result: kafka-kraft-controller-1 enters CrashLoopBackOff (RESTARTS: 47 after 27 minutes). The remaining 2-node quorum (nodes 0 and 2) elects node 0 as leader and maintains all 360 existing topic partition replications. However, KRaft requires a committed majority (2-of-3 voters) to apply any controller-plane log entry — since node 1 is cycling rather than voting, ALL AdminClient mutation operations block for 30s then return:

  org.apache.kafka.common.errors.TimeoutError: Timed out waiting for a node assignment. Call: createTopics

All 14 microservices in a staging→production canary deploy call auto.create.topics.enable=true at startup via the Python confluent-kafka 2.3 SDK. Each hangs 30s, fails its Kubernetes readinessProbe, and stalls the deploy. 8 Celery worker pods for the ML feature pipeline also fail startup (topic ml-feature-events-v4 cannot be created). Confluent Schema Registry (backed by the _schemas Kafka topic) blocks new Avro schema registrations:

  ERROR Schema registration failed: [HTTP 408] Timeout waiting for topic _schemas leadership confirmation (io.confluent.kafka.schemaregistry.storage.KafkaStore)

Existing consumers and producers on all 360 established topics are completely unaffected — partition leader election and ISR management continue normally. The 22-minute misdiagnosis: the on-call spends time checking broker-to-broker connectivity and JVM heap (both healthy) before noticing the KRaft-specific CrashLoopBackOff.
items.exercise:
1. Triage — check KRaft quorum status, not broker health: Run kafka-metadata-quorum.sh --bootstrap-server kafka-broker-0.kafka-brokers-headless:9092 describe --status. During the outage: LeaderId: 0, LeaderEpoch: 47, HighWatermark: 891204, CurrentVoters: [0, 2] — voter 1 is missing. This immediately implicates the controller replacement. Confirm the CrashLoopBackOff: kubectl get pods -n kafka | grep controller → kafka-kraft-controller-1 shows RESTARTS=47. Read the fatal log: kubectl logs kafka-kraft-controller-1 -n kafka --previous | grep -E 'FATAL|meta.properties|cluster.id' — 'cluster.id not present' confirms the new PVC is empty and unformatted. Rule out broker issues: kubectl exec kafka-kraft-controller-0 -n kafka -- kafka-broker-api-versions.sh --bootstrap-server kafka-broker-0:9092 — returns broker API versions normally, confirming all 9 brokers are healthy and the failure is isolated to the controller-plane mutation path.
2. Retrieve the cluster ID from a surviving controller: The cluster.id written to meta.properties during initial cluster bootstrap must be identical across all three controller nodes. Retrieve it: kubectl exec kafka-kraft-controller-0 -n kafka -- cat /var/lib/kafka/data/meta.properties → cluster.id=MkU3OEVBNTcwNTJENDM2Qk. Alternatively: kafka-metadata-quorum.sh ... describe --status | grep ClusterId. Keep this UUID — it is required for the next step and is irretrievable after the original PVC is gone.
3. Re-initialize the replacement controller storage and restart: Exec into the new kafka-kraft-controller-1 pod and run kafka-storage.sh format with the retrieved cluster UUID: kubectl exec -it kafka-kraft-controller-1 -n kafka -- kafka-storage.sh format --config /etc/kafka/controller.properties --cluster-id MkU3OEVBNTcwNTJENDM2Qk --ignore-formatted. The --ignore-formatted flag is safe because the storage IS empty; it writes a valid meta.properties: {version=1, cluster.id=MkU3OEVBNTcwNTJENDM2Qk, node.id=1}. Delete the pod to trigger a clean restart (Kubernetes terminates the current CrashLoopBackOff cycle immediately): kubectl delete pod kafka-kraft-controller-1 -n kafka. The StatefulSet recreates it; on startup it reads the newly written meta.properties, announces voter ID 1 to the quorum, and joins within 5–8 seconds.
4. Validate quorum rejoin and unblock AdminClient: Within 15 seconds of the pod restarting, run kafka-metadata-quorum.sh describe --status again — CurrentVoters should show [0, 1, 2] and LeaderId should be stable (no re-election needed). Verify mutation operations are unblocked: kafka-topics.sh --bootstrap-server kafka-broker-0:9092 --create --topic test-recovery-$(date +%s) --partitions 1 --replication-factor 3 → 'Created topic test-recovery-<timestamp>' in < 1s. Check Schema Registry: curl -s http://schema-registry.kafka.svc:8081/subjects — returns the subjects list (was 408 timeouts during the outage). Confirm the 14 stalled microservice deploys progress within 60s as their startup topic creation succeeds.
5. Fix the controller replacement procedure — snapshot before PVC deletion: The correct KRaft controller PVC replacement procedure: (a) If the pod is healthy but PVC is damaged, cordon the pod first and take a VolumeSnapshot before deletion. (b) If PVC must be deleted: NEVER delete both PVC and pod simultaneously. Delete the pod, wait for Kubernetes to create a new pod with the OLD PVC still mounted (even if degraded), run kafka-storage.sh format on a NEW PVC while the old pod is still running, then swap. (c) Simplest safe procedure: delete ONLY the pod (not the PVC) — Kubernetes StatefulSets preserve the PVC across pod restarts; the existing meta.properties survives. Critically document the ZK-vs-KRaft difference: in ZooKeeper mode, Kafka stored zero critical controller state on the broker disk (ZK held all of it), so PVC-delete-and-recreate was safe. In KRaft mode, the controller disk holds the entire metadata log — it is as critical as an etcd data volume.
6. Prevent: (a) Add an OPA/Gatekeeper admission webhook that blocks PVC deletion for StatefulSet pods in the kafka namespace unless a matching VolumeSnapshot exists within the past 15 minutes — the webhook surfaces a 'kafka-kraft-controller PVCs require a snapshot before deletion' error message, stopping the wrong runbook step before it executes. (b) Add a Kafka metric alert: kafka_controller_quorum_voter_count < 3 for > 30s → PagerDuty P1 'KRaft voter missing' — this fires the instant a controller leaves the voter set, 90s faster than the leader_id==-1 alert. (c) Update the ZooKeeper-mode controller replacement runbook to explicitly warn 'DO NOT follow this procedure for KRaft mode — PVC deletion is destructive; see the KRaft runbook instead.' Cross-link from the Kafka StatefulSets troubleshooting wiki. (d) Run a quarterly KRaft disaster drill in staging: replace one controller PVC using the correct snapshot-based procedure, measuring quorum rejoin time (target: < 90s). This builds operator muscle memory before a midnight PagerDuty page in production.

items.drill: Nomad Job Priority Inversion — ML Training Batch priority=100 Preempts All 24 Payment Service Allocations, $1.54M Revenue Halt in 22 Minutes
items.layer: Distributed Systems (HashiCorp Nomad 1.7.6 / Go / AWS EC2 / Consul / Envoy)
items.scenario:
At 11:08 UTC, PagerDuty fires: 'payment-api-prod availability 0% (threshold: 99%)'. Your Go 1.22 payment service (24 allocations, HashiCorp Nomad 1.7.6, 12× AWS EC2 c6i.4xlarge service nodes, us-east-1) processes $4.2M/hour at 4,800 RPS. At 11:06:22 UTC, a data scientist deploys ml-training-sweep — a Nomad hyperparameter sweep batch job (80 task groups × 2 CPU / 4 GB each) cloned from the dev cluster. During last month's dev→prod cluster migration, all job specs were bulk-imported with their original priority values. The dev cluster had assigned priority=100 to all ML training jobs to ensure they ran completely on the under-utilized dev hardware.

The payment service runs at priority=80 — correct for production services in isolation, but now the lowest-priority job on a cluster dominated by priority=100 ML training specs. Nomad 1.7.6 preemption is enabled globally (preemption_config.batch_scheduler_enabled=true, set during cluster setup for GPU allocation efficiency). The cluster runs at 87% capacity. With 80 task groups needing 160 CPU and only 22 CPUs free, Nomad's preemption algorithm selects payment-api allocations as victims — they yield the most CPU per eviction (2 CPU each) at the lowest priority:

  2026-06-25T11:06:33.441Z [INFO]  nomad.scheduler.batch: preempting alloc a7f3c8d2
    job=payment-api taskGroup=api priority=80
    preempted_by_job=ml-training-sweep preempted_by_priority=100
    reason=insufficient_cpu freed_cpu=2000
  2026-06-25T11:06:33.444Z [INFO]  nomad.scheduler.batch: preempting alloc b9e1f0c4
    job=payment-api taskGroup=api priority=80 preempted_by_job=ml-training-sweep
  [22 additional identical lines within 2 seconds — all 24 payment-api allocs stopped]

All 24 payment-api allocations receive SIGTERM simultaneously at 11:06:35 UTC. Each Go binary drains in-flight requests and exits in <500ms. The Nomad scheduler immediately attempts to reschedule all 24 payment-api allocations, but cannot: ml-training-sweep (priority=100) now occupies all freed capacity and re-preempts any payment-api allocation the instant it is placed. The scheduler enters a priority-inversion death cycle: the rescheduling loop runs every 3 seconds and ejects payment-api each time it finds a slot.

Consul deregisters all payment-api service instances within 10 seconds (health check interval=5s, deregister_critical_service_after=10s). Envoy upstream cluster empties:

  2026-06-25T11:06:55.031Z [WARN] consul-agent: service payment-api:8080 health check CRITICAL (TCP refused)
  2026-06-25T11:06:57.112Z [ERROR] envoy: no healthy upstream for cluster payment-api.service.consul (0/0 endpoints)
  2026-06-25T11:07:01.884Z [ERROR] payment-gateway: upstream connect error or disconnect/reset before headers — reset reason: connection failure

Nomad dashboard (11:06-11:30 UTC):
  payment-api pending allocs: 24  (re-preempted each 3s rescheduling cycle)
  payment-api running allocs: 0
  ml-training-sweep running:  80
  cluster CPU utilization:    97.4%
  payment-api preemption count: 24 initial + 76 re-preemptions during 22-min window

On-call spent 11 minutes checking node health, network connectivity, and Go application logs before noticing Nomad's alloc stop_reason=preempted in the allocation detail view. Business impact: payment API fully unavailable for 22 minutes. $4.2M/hr × 22/60 = $1.54M failed transaction volume. 312K user checkout sessions returned HTTP 503. CS queue peaked at 7,200 tickets. 3 merchant payment SLA breaches (guaranteed 99.95% monthly uptime).
items.exercise:
1. Identify preemption as root cause (11 minutes saved): nomad job status payment-api — all 24 allocations in 'pending' with reason column showing 'preempted'. Click any alloc in Nomad UI → 'Preempted By: ml-training-sweep (priority 100 vs payment-api priority 80)'. CLI cross-check: nomad alloc status -json <any-alloc-id> | jq .DesiredDescription → 'Preempted by higher priority job ml-training-sweep'. Confirm the re-preemption loop: watch -n3 'nomad job status payment-api | grep pending | wc -l' — count oscillates between 24 (unscheduled) and 0 for a fraction of a second before being evicted again. Run nomad node status -allocs <node-id> on any cluster node: ml-training-sweep task groups occupy 96% of CPU, payment-api is absent. This is a scheduler configuration failure — all 12 nodes are healthy and online.
2. Immediate mitigation — stop the batch job: nomad job stop ml-training-sweep. Nomad terminates all 80 task groups within 15 seconds (batch jobs receive SIGKILL after 5s drain, no graceful handling expected for training code). Resources freed.
